EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms with strong damaging winds tracked across northeast Colorado during the afternoon and evening of May 14th. High winds of 60-80 mph reported in numerous locations, and at least a couple reports of trees that fell onto homes. One in Brush, CO, and two more homes had damage due to fallen trees in Holyoke, CO. Power outages were reported in Holyoke, Iliff, Brush, and some surrounding rural locations in northeast Colorado. Strong winds from a cold front (enhanced from the thunderstorm outflow winds) pushed through eastern Elbert and Lincoln Counties with gusts of 55 to 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured with Vantage Pro Weather Station. Also reported 3-4 inch diameter tree limbs down and minor roof damage to outbuilding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked to the north of the region during the day on the 16th, with an associated front draped across northeast Utah and northwest Colorado. An upper level jet streak was tracking across region during this period as well, providing some extra lift. Instability in the realm of 600-800 J/kg of CAPE was also present across this region, as well as ample moisture. All these combined to fire off strong to severe thunderstorms across northeast Utah and northwest Colorado during the afternoon hours. While this set up was not as potent as that seen during the 11th to the 13th, it was enough to cause some flash flooding and severe thunderstorm winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported mud over State Highway 64 near Mile Marker 65. This area is near the Lee Burn Scar, which received several rounds of heavy rain during this event. CDOT was contacted to clear the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Derby Fire started in mid August due to a lightning strike 13.5 miles north of Dotsero, Colorado. The fire grew to 5,453 acres spreading across Eagle and Garfield counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Derby Fire started in mid August due to a lightning strike 13.5 miles north of Dotsero, Colorado. The fire grew to 5,453 acres spreading across Eagle and Garfield counties. While major suppression efforts ended in October and significant fire activity ceased, the remaining 5 percent was in an inaccessible area with pockets of interior smoke. It was officially 100 percent contained by mid December. The estimated cost was $23,860,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The period between the 11th and 13th of September had a relatively unique meteorological setup for western Colorado and eastern Utah. A potent closed low was located over Southern California, and a ridge of high pressure was located to our southeast, directing a rich plume of monsoonal moisture northward through the Four Corners. Mixing ratios in this plume of moisture were more than high enough to not only help form strong to severe thunderstorms, but also to produce flash flooding and debris flows. Additionally, there was ample instability, with morning clearing helping 600-1000 J/kg of CAPE develop each afternoon. The region also fell under the right exit region of a potent upper level jet streak, which added additional lift and 40-50 kts of shear. Additionally, terrain effects added additional lift and shear. All together, this was a potent setup with the potential to produce severe thunderstorms. And indeed, the storms that developed produced not only large hail and strong, damaging winds, but also two tornadoes in Southeast Utah, and flash flooding across the area. Particularly, fresh burn scars, including the Lee and Elk RBX scars, saw substantial flash flooding and debris flows as storms trained over the area and produced very heavy rain. Finally, after 3 days of widespread severe weather, the cold front pushed through the region, and a cooler, drier, and above all, calmer airmass moved in, bringing an end to the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported street flooding at the intersection of State Route 340 and Rimrock Drive, near the west entrance to Colorado National Monument. No reports of road closures or damages due to this flooding were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The period between the 11th and 13th of September had a relatively unique meteorological setup for western Colorado and eastern Utah. A potent closed low was located over Southern California, and a ridge of high pressure was located to our southeast, directing a rich plume of monsoonal moisture northward through the Four Corners. Mixing ratios in this plume of moisture were more than high enough to not only help form strong to severe thunderstorms, but also to produce flash flooding and debris flows. Additionally, there was ample instability, with morning clearing helping 600-1000 J/kg of CAPE develop each afternoon. The region also fell under the right exit region of a potent upper level jet streak, which added additional lift and 40-50 kts of shear. Additionally, terrain effects added additional lift and shear. All together, this was a potent setup with the potential to produce severe thunderstorms. And indeed, the storms that developed produced not only large hail and strong, damaging winds, but also two tornadoes in Southeast Utah, and flash flooding across the area. Particularly, fresh burn scars, including the Lee and Elk RBX scars, saw substantial flash flooding and debris flows as storms trained over the area and produced very heavy rain. Finally, after 3 days of widespread severe weather, the cold front pushed through the region, and a cooler, drier, and above all, calmer airmass moved in, bringing an end to the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported mud and debris on State Route 8 due to heavy rain on the recent Elk RBX Burn Scar. No reports of damages, injuries, or fatalities, just clearing of the roadway.

CZ_NAME: S & SE GRAND / W CENTRAL & SW BOULDER / GILPIN / CLEAR CREEK / SUMMIT / N & W PARK COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread rain and snow fell across eastern Colorado with the snow level falling to 7000 to 8000 feet MSL. The heaviest snowfall occurred over the southern foothills and the Front Range mountains. Roads were snow-covered in the high country during the morning hours of May 7th. Westbound Interstate 70 was closed for a short period on the morning of May 7th morning near Georgetown. Main impacts from the storm were snow covered and slippery roads in the higher foothills and mountains late on the 6th and during the morning of the 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reports show 12-20 inches in the mountains of Clear Creek and Gilpin Counties. Roads were snow covered and slippery late on the 6th and during the morning of the 7th. I-70 was closed west of Georgetown briefly on the morning of the 7th. Other than the snow covered roads, no other issues were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms with strong damaging winds tracked across northeast Colorado during the afternoon and evening of May 14th. High winds of 60-80 mph reported in numerous locations, and at least a couple reports of trees that fell onto homes. One in Brush, CO, and two more homes had damage due to fallen trees in Holyoke, CO. Power outages were reported in Holyoke, Iliff, Brush, and some surrounding rural locations in northeast Colorado. Strong winds from a cold front (enhanced from the thunderstorm outflow winds) pushed through eastern Elbert and Lincoln Counties with gusts of 55 to 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power poles down near the intersection of County Road 25 and County Road R. Time estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A plume of anomalously rich monsoonal moisture pushed into eastern Utah and western Colorado in the days prior to these debris flows. On the day of the flows, precipitable water values were in excess of 1.00 inch across the region. Daytime heating acting on the complex terrain of the region allowed for the formation of thunderstorms during the afternoon. These storms were capable of producing heavy rainfall with very high rain rates. Over the course of the day, multiple of these storms moved over the recent Lee and Elk RBX burn scars in Rio Blanco county, triggering multiple debris flows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This incident was reported to the NWS Grand Junction office by the NWS Incident Meteorologist assigned to the Lee Wildfire. Debris flowed across County Road 5 from due to heavy rain on the adjacent Lee burn scar. A tractor trailer attempted to drive through the debris flow and became stuck. The truck was eventually unstuck and the road was cleared. No damages or injuries or fatalities were reported.

This flash flooding began within the Lee Burn Scar, and washed down over Rio Blanco County Road 33. This flooding was not reported to have caused significant damage, but did prevent residents living along County Road 33 from getting to or from their homes until the water retreated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: August 23rd marked the beginning of a push of unusually rich tropical moisture that would plague eastern Utah and western Colorado for the next week. At this point, much of the convective initiation was down to daytime heating on the complex terrain of the region. Heavy rain was not as widespread of a concern as it would be in the following days, but was still possible with stronger storms or in areas of training thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At approximately 3:20 PM MDT, local law enforcement reported a rockfall between mile-markers 115 and 116 on Highway 141 in Unaweep Canyon, roughly 4 miles east of the town of Gateway. The Turner Gulch burn scar, which is adjacent to this location, experienced some heavy rain prior to this event. This did result in the need for the road to be cleaned, although no reports of the road being closed specifically due to this rockfall were received. Additionally, no reports of damages, injuries, or fatalities were received.

From the Denver Post...through 6:45 PM Wednesday, 939 flights at DIA were delayed and 11 were canceled, according to flight-tracking website FlightAware. Several school districts delayed opening due to treacherous travel conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unusually rich tropical moisture continued to stream over eastern Utah and western Colorado, with precipitable water values in excess of an inch. Combination of upper level support in the form of a jet streak and daytime heating on complex terrain provided the lift to start showers and thunderstorms. These storms, as had been the case in the days prior, were capable of producing heavy rain and high rain rates. Over sensitive terrain, such as slot canyons or recent burn scars, this could cause flash flooding and debris flows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NWS Incident Meteorologist assigned to the Lee Fire reported this event to NWS Grand Junction. Mud and debris were confirmed to be flowing across Rio Blanco County Road 5 at mile marker 6 and mile marker 12, making the road impassable. As a result, County Road 5 was closed from miles marker 12 to the intersection with Highway 13 for roughly half an hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong weather system developed on March 4th and brought very strong winds to Cheyenne, Kit Carson, and Yuma counties. The strongest wind gust was 86 mph. Additional wind gusts of 60 to 75 mph were also recorded throughout the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported a picture of their personal weather station showing a wind gust of 85.6 mph 12 N Firstview. A mesonet station named UP694 2.2 E Arapahoe also reported wind gusts ranging from 58 to 82 mph throughout the day as well. Another report of 75 mph winds came from the public as well in Cheyenne Wells showing a 72 and 75 mph wind gust on their personal weather station. Another mesonet station in Firstview named CO068 040w460 U40-Firstview also reported a 68 and 70 mph wind gust. Another mesonet station named UP695 Aroya (UPR) in Aroya reported a 61 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The South Rim Fire started in early July due to multiple lightning strikes 9 miles east of Montrose, Colorado which ignited fires on both the north and south rim of the Black Canyon of the Gunnison National Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The South Rim Fire started in early July due to multiple lightning strikes 9 miles east of Montrose, Colorado which ignited fires on both the north and south rim of the Black Canyon of the Gunnison National Park. The South Rim Fire became the larger of the two, which grew to 4,232 acres before it was contained in early to mid September. The North Rim Fire was quickly put out one day after it started on July 11th. The north rim of the park was reopened to the public July 30th, while the south rim reopened to the public on August 18th. The estimated cost to date was $15,300,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Lee Fire started in early August due to a lightning strike 20 miles southwest of Meeker, Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lee Fire started in early August due to a lightning strike 20 miles southwest of Meeker, Colorado. The Grease Fire grew into the Lee Fire on August 4 and acreage was combined and further known as the Lee Fire. The fire grew to 137,758 acres before it was contained in mid September. The estimated cost to date was $30,106,091. At least five homes and 14 additional structures, such as outbuildings were destroyed by the Lee Fire. The Lee Fire became the fifth largest wildfire in Colorado history. Despite the damage, no injuries or deaths were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drier air working in from the west with monsoonal moisture remaining along the Divide resulted in some stronger storms developing across the higher terrain of western Colorado on the edge of this moisture and dry air gradient. 35 to 45 knots of shear was present to allow a few storms to produce pea to dime size hail with a few nickel size hailstones in Hinsdale County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong storm developed 7 miles south-southwest of Lake City producing pea to dime size hail with a few nickel size hailstones around 1045 MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced severe wind gusts across eastern Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a 53 knot gusts measured at the LAA asos.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked to the north of the region during the day on the 16th, with an associated front draped across northeast Utah and northwest Colorado. An upper level jet streak was tracking across region during this period as well, providing some extra lift. Instability in the realm of 600-800 J/kg of CAPE was also present across this region, as well as ample moisture. All these combined to fire off strong to severe thunderstorms across northeast Utah and northwest Colorado during the afternoon hours. While this set up was not as potent as that seen during the 11th to the 13th, it was enough to cause some flash flooding and severe thunderstorm winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 5:44 PM MDT, the KRIL ASOS at Rifle - Garfield County Regional Airport recorded a gust to 60 mph. Other sensors in the area at the time recorded wind gusts of 45 mph to 55 mph. These wind gusts were all associated with a passing thunderstorm, though no damages, fatalities, or injuries were reported in association with it.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system strengthened over eastern Colorado during the morning hours of January 30th. This brought snow showers to the eastern plains and southern part of the Denver area. A band of moderate to heavy snow brought localized snowfall amounts of up to 8 inches, which created difficult travel on I-70 east of Denver.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports widely varied due to banded snowfall across the area with less than an inch across western parts of the zone and up to 8 inches across the east. Slow travel was noted on I-25 during the morning hours. Here are a few of the heavier snowfall reports, 8 inches 3 south of Kiowa, 7 inches near Agate, 6 inches 7 miles southwest of Deer Trail, and 4 inches near Elizabeth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the days prior to this avalanche, which caught and seriously injured a backcountry skier, a strong winter storm brought strong winds and fresh snow the terrain. This accident occurred in an area south of Marble, CO at an elevation of 10,400 feet. This puts it near to above treeline. On the day of the accident, the Colorado Avalanche Information Center (CAIC) rated the avalanche danger, both near and above treeline, as Considerable (3/5), with areas below treeline at Moderate (2/5). The highest avalanche danger was on north-facing slopes and at steep slope angles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This injurious avalanche occurred south of the town of Marble, CO, off of a terrain feature know as Raspberry Ridge, in a run known locally as Money Shot. The Colorado Avalanche Information Center (CAIC) rated the avalanche danger in this area as Considerable (3/5) near and above treeline. CAIC's synopsis said dangerous avalanche conditions exist and indicated that slopes with a northerly component were the most dangerous, with the possibility of triggering a deadly avalanche on any of these slopes. Below treeline, the avalanche danger was listed as Moderate (2/5). The avalanche types of concern were Persistent Slab and Wind Slab. The accident slope on the 8th was steep, high elevation, and faced northeast, all factors that, per CAIC's forecast, made this a dangerous to deadly slope. In addition, this slope was known to be significantly more shallow than nearby slopes, and had a history of avalanching multiple times earlier in the 2024/2025 winter season. In fact, just the day before two backcountry tourers triggered two small avalanches. One of the March 7th avalanches caught one of the backcountry tourers, but they were uninjured. The other avalanche was triggered remotely and broke just below the March 8th accident site.||A full summary of this accident was unavailable. The synopsis given, via CAIC, indicates that a skier triggered and was subsequently caught, carried, and seriously injured by, an avalanche. The skier's partner assisted in digging the skier out of the snow and addressing the injuries as best as possible. The partner then dragged the skier down nearly 600 vertical feet to a trail intersection, where local search and rescue teams were waiting. The skier was then transported to Aspen Valley Hospital via helicopter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep closed low pressure system moved through the Great Basin into the Four Corners region on the heels of another system. This low brought deep Atmospheric River moisture from the Desert Southwest through the Four Corners ahead of it, along with impressive dynamics and a strong jet to produce moderate to heavy snowfall across most mountain ranges of eastern Utah and western Colorado where the highest amounts of 1 to 2 feet of snow fell across the mountains of eastern Utah and southwest Colorado. Temperatures remained mild enough that most lower valleys saw primarily rain, limiting snowfall accumulations.  Winds were quite strong as well with gusts in the mountains in the 60 to 80 mph range on average, leading to impacts from blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 7 to 14 inches of snow fell across the area with up to 18 inches at Tower. Peak wind gust of 66 mph occurred at Storm Peak Lab.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system was approaching eastern Utah and western Colorado from the west on the 6ht, bringing mountain snow and valley rain showers. In addition, as this low approached, bringing with it a potent cold font, south to southwesterly winds strengthened through the day. Much of the region saw wind gusts 40-50 mph, with a few gusts reaching 60-70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak gust of 64 mph was recorded at the Hallenbeck Reservoir mesonet site at approximately 8:00 AM MST. In addition, a gust to 60 mph was recorded at the Colorado department of transportation mesonet site near the Utah state line just before 3:00 PM MST. Gusts across the zone ranged from 50 mph to the peak of 65 mph. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As is typical for spring high wind events, a strong low pressure was approaching eastern Utah and western Colorado from the west, bringing a strong cold front and upper level jet along with it. In this case, the upper level jet was primarily over areas of northeast Utah and northwest Colorado, and it is this area that saw the strongest wind gusts ahead of the cold front. That said, winds gusting 45-55 mph were common across the region, with gusts of 65-75 mph recorded at the higher elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The KCAG ASOS in Craig, CO recorded a gust to 61 mph at approximately 5:30 PM MDT, likely associated with the cold front passage, as no high gusts are recorded after this point. Prior to this point, wind gusts of 45-55 mph were recorded across the zone. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving cold front and shortwave trough moved through eastern Utah and western Colorado, bringing moderate snowfall across most mountain areas of eastern Utah and western Colorado. The higher amounts fell across the mountains of southeast Utah and southwest Colorado due to favorable orographics and better available moisture. In addition, strong winds led to blowing snow across the mountain areas. Light to moderate snowfall fell across some valley locations including portions of the southwest Colorado valley foothills as well as central mountain corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 7 to 12 inches of snow fell across area. Winds generally gusted in the 45 to 60 mph range at elevations above 12,000 feet with a peak wind gust of 63 mph at Telluride Ski Resort Gold Hill on March 14 at 1:00.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure trough and associated cold front brought mild air ahead of it and much colder air behind it to result in light to moderate snowfall across most mountain areas of eastern Utah and western Colorado with rain changing over to snow in many lower valley locations even though accumulations remained light. Heavier snowfall seemed to favor the La Sal Mountains of southeast Utah as well as the northwest San Juans and Grand Mesa mainly due to the colder northerly flow behind the cold front. Winds were also fairly strong leading to blowing and drifting snow in some mountain areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 5 to 13 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts up to 18 inches at El Diente Peak. Winds generally gusted in the 45 to 70 mph range at elevations above 10,000 feet with a peak wind gust of 78 mph at Kendall Mountain. There was a reported gust of 112 mph at Eagle on March 18 at 11:00 but it seems suspect based on surrounding reports.

Heavier snowfall seemed to favor the La Sal Mountains of southeast Utah as well as the northwest San Juans and Grand Mesa mainly due to the colder northerly flow behind the cold front. Winds were also fairly strong leading to blowing and drifting snow in some mountain areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 8 inches of snow fell across the area with 7 to 8 inches at Columbine Pass and 4 to 6 inches around the Ridgway area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The period between the 11th and 13th of September had a relatively unique meteorological setup for western Colorado and eastern Utah. A potent closed low was located over Southern California, and a ridge of high pressure was located to our southeast, directing a rich plume of monsoonal moisture northward through the Four Corners. Mixing ratios in this plume of moisture were more than high enough to not only help form strong to severe thunderstorms, but also to produce flash flooding and debris flows. Additionally, there was ample instability, with morning clearing helping 600-1000 J/kg of CAPE develop each afternoon. The region also fell under the right exit region of a potent upper level jet streak, which added additional lift and 40-50 kts of shear. Additionally, terrain effects added additional lift and shear. All together, this was a potent setup with the potential to produce severe thunderstorms. And indeed, the storms that developed produced not only large hail and strong, damaging winds, but also two tornadoes in Southeast Utah, and flash flooding across the area. Particularly, fresh burn scars, including the Lee and Elk RBX scars, saw substantial flash flooding and debris flows as storms trained over the area and produced very heavy rain. Finally, after 3 days of widespread severe weather, the cold front pushed through the region, and a cooler, drier, and above all, calmer airmass moved in, bringing an end to the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The CoCoRaHS reported at Mack 5NW called in a report of a 61 mph wind gust at her location in association with a passing thunderstorm. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received in association with this event.

CZ_NAME: S & SE GRAND / W CENTRAL & SW BOULDER / GILPIN / CLEAR CREEK / SUMMIT / N & W PARK COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow began late on the 10th and continued most of the 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: U.S. 6 closed between east of Keystone Resort, to Interstate 70, with CDOT officials reporting slick and icy conditions. The road was still reportedly icy and snow-packed as of when it opened around 10am on the 11th, with multiple snow plows active in the area. Snotel sites and ski resorts reported 3 to 11 inches of snow fell across the area, with the heaviest snow falling around the Copper Mountain and Breckenridge areas. This also led to slow and slippery travel on I-70 through Summit County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 3:06 PM MDT, NWS Grand Junction received a report from local law enforcement that powerlines had been downed in nearby Fruita, CO in association with a passing severe thunderstorm. Six minutes later, this storm reached the Grand Junction Regional Airport, where the KGJT ASOS recorded a wind gust to 68 mph. No other damages were reported with this event, and no reports of injuries or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported from a strong thunderstorm moving north across Cheyenne County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report sent in via mPING.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong supercell thunderstorm tracked south-southeast across far northeast Weld, southwest Logan and northwest Washington Counties. The storms produced large hail, up to 3 inches in diameter and also three tornadoes across Logan and Washington Counties during the afternoon hours of May 23rd, including one EF-2 and one EF-1. Most of the observed damage was snapped power poles and trees. One single family residence sustained EF-1 damage, but there were no injuries reported. This supercell also produced a swath of golf ball to baseball sized hail, with one ICECHIP report of over 3 inch diameter hail in northern Washington county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report from Mping.

The fire grew to 298 acres before it was contained in early August. The estimated cost to date was $4,998,587.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning of the 14th, northwesterly winds began speeding up as a cold front moved across the region from the northwest to the east. Eastern Colorado saw winds up to 85 MPH that lofted dust, causing visibilities to drop to around 200 feet in Cheyenne county. U.S. Highway 40 and Interstate 70 were both shut down due to the hazardous conditions. Conditions began to improve in the early to mid-afternoon. Scattered storms also occurred and assisted in 70 MPH winds reaching the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of visibility less than 1/4 mile in heavy blowing dust, sometimes dropping to around 200 feet. U.S. Highway 40 was shut down due to the dust storm. This blowing dust occurred in concert with the high winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts mainly ranged from 2 to 6 inches across the area. Localized heavier snow of 9 to 12 inches fell in the Boulder area. This led to snow covered and slick roads.

CZ_NAME: SE ELBERT COUNTY BELOW 6000 FEET / SOUTH LINCOLN COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system developed over the Central Plains and brought strong winds to the eastern plains and snow to the mountains/Palmer Divide which produced snow covered roads. The storm system was slightly weaker and formed farther east then forecasted, resulting in less precipitation and warmer temperatures that produce mainly rain over the plains. The strongest winds were found over the plains where southern Lincoln County saw a wind gust to 81 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 81 mph was measured 8 miles north-northwest of Boyero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning of the 14th, northwesterly winds began speeding up as a cold front moved across the region from the northwest to the east. Eastern Colorado saw winds up to 85 MPH that lofted dust, causing visibilities to drop to around 200 feet in Cheyenne county. U.S. Highway 40 and Interstate 70 were both shut down due to the hazardous conditions. Conditions began to improve in the early to mid-afternoon. Scattered storms also occurred and assisted in 70 MPH winds reaching the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As convection moved overhead, the Burlington ASOS reported gusts up to 72 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm system moved across Eastern Colorado, beginning during the evening of the 18th.  Rain changed to snow as winds turned from the northwest to the north and increased.  This led to blizzard conditions moving in from the west.  Snowfall amounts ranged from trace to around four inches, with the highest amounts in Yuma County.  Wind gusts up to 74 MPH were reported, with the highest gust occurring near Kit Carson.  Ground blizzard conditions persisted after the snow ended.  Some highways were shut down due to the blizzard.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to four inches of snow was reported across the county, with the highest amounts over the northeast part of the county.  Wind gusts of 60 MPH or greater occurred from 10:30 PM through 11 AM the next day.  The highest wind gust reported was 59 MPH at 10:53 AM at the Burlington airport.  Some highways were closed due to the blizzard.||Key impacts:  whiteout conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front brought cold temperatures and snowfall to the area beginning late on the 17th and continuing into the 18th. The snowfall and cold temperatures led to snow covered roads.||A second and colder arctic cold front brought colder temperatures and light snowfall to the area on the 19th and 20th.||Denver's sheltering capacity was stretched. They cared for at least 1600 people in a variety of contexts Saturday night (18th), and more are expected Sunday (19th) and Monday nights (20th). No one has been turned away. Because of the cold temperatures, the annual Martin Luther King Jr Day march was canceled.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on reports, 4 to 9 inches of snow fell across the the area. Because of the snow and cold temperatures, roads became snow covered and slippery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread snow fell across the Front Range Mountains and Foothills as well across the urban corridor. Temperatures remained near or above freezing for most of the event below 6000 feet MSL leading to lighter snow accumulation and snow melting on roadways. The strong April sun help keep roads in the mountains and foothills mainly wet during the daylight hours. Roads  became slippery during the nighttime hours in the mountains and foothills. Crashes and safety closures were reported on I-70 and US 6.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snotel and Cocorahs observations showed 4 to 11 inches of snow fell across the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Main impact was blowing and drifting snow across the plains of eastern Colorado where wind gusts to around 50 mph reduced visibility to less than a quarter mile at times. Snow did fall as well, but due to the wind gusts accurate measurements were very difficult to make by weather observers/spotters. The blowing snow cause I-70 to be closed from just east of Denver to the Kansas state line. Colorado State Patrol officials out of Limon said nearly all major highways between Burlington and E-470 near DIA are closed because of the weather. Side roads are in even worse shape and shouldn���t be used to get around the closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observations from spotters and CoCoRahs showed 1 to 5 inches of snow fell across the area. Due to the gusty north winds, it was very difficult to accurately measure snowfall. The snow and gusty winds produced snow covered and slippery roads, including I-25.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The following highways are closed all the way to the Kansas border due to weather conditions: I-70, US-385, US-40/287, Us-24, CO-94, CO-86, CO-59, CO-71. Parts of I-25 were closed last night in Douglas county due to blowing snow. Multiple crashes involving semi-trucks, and many spun-out-vehicles. Elbert Count School District 200 closed for Wednesday. At least 8 other school districts in eastern Colorado had a delayed start of 2 hours. Thousands are without power due to strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: I-25 near Monument and CO-86 from Elizabeth to Limon were closed due to blizzard conditions. Monument Hill AWOS showed blizzard conditions before the visibility sensor went out. Numerous Cocorahs observers also reported blizzard conditions on the evening on the 18th, including a 64 mph wind report 4.1 miles northeast of Elizabeth.

CZ_NAME: BOULDER & JEFFERSON COUNTIES BELOW 6000 FEET / W BROOMFIELD COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds of 50 to 70 mph occurred in the Front Range foothills and far west side of the Denver metro area. There were isolated reported in the 70 to 80 mph range with a peak wind of 82 mph measured at 3 miles southwest Rocky Flats. The large power outages (20,000 total customers) in the West Metro area were caused by broken equipment, likely caused by the high winds this morning, Xcel Energy said.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In addition to being part of the 20,000 power outages, wind gusted to 50 to 70 mph for a brief time between 0400-0500 on the 24th. The peak gust reached 73 mph at the Arvada RAWS weather station. A few other gusts reach 55 to 63 mph near Boulder.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning of the 14th, northwesterly winds began speeding up as a cold front moved across the region from the northwest to the east. Eastern Colorado saw winds up to 85 MPH that lofted dust, causing visibilities to drop to around 200 feet in Cheyenne county. U.S. Highway 40 and Interstate 70 were both shut down due to the hazardous conditions. Conditions began to improve in the early to mid-afternoon. Scattered storms also occurred and assisted in 70 MPH winds reaching the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds began gusting over 58 MPH just before 10 AM LST and continued until just before 3 PM LST. The peak recorded wind gust was 60 MPH at 1130 LST from the Burlington ASOS. Blowing dust did result from these strong winds causing Interstate 70 to be closed, but no visibilities below 1/2 mile were reported. Stronger winds were reported at the Burlington ASOS, however they occurred along with convection and will be entered as such in other events.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front ushered in an arctic airmass on the evening of January 17th, as well as bringing widespread wind and snow to eastern Utah and western Colorado. This arctic airmass was reinforced over the course of the next week by several passing waves with their own associated cold fronts. Temperatures during this period were 15-20 degrees below normal, and in some places close to 30 degrees below normal. With temperatures this cold, any amount of wind would produce a dangerously cold wind chill. The coldest period of this event was January 20th to the 22nd, with temperatures finally beginning to moderate after the 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The KEGE AWOS in Eagle recorded an approximately 1.5 hour period where temperatures were below 25F and wind speeds were sufficient to produce wind chill values below 15F. Winds were below the 10 mph threshold, however they produced wind chills cold enough to warrant recording. Temperatures ranged from 0F to 1F, and wind speeds ranged from 6 mph to 7 mph. The coldest wind chill value recorded was -13F. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front ushered in an arctic airmass on the evening of January 17th, as well as bringing widespread wind and snow to eastern Utah and western Colorado. This arctic airmass was reinforced over the course of the next week by several passing waves with their own associated cold fronts. Temperatures during this period were 15-20 degrees below normal, and in some places close to 30 degrees below normal. With temperatures this cold, any amount of wind would produce a dangerously cold wind chill. The coldest period of this event was January 20th to the 22nd, with temperatures finally beginning to moderate after the 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The KEGE AWOS in Eagle recorded an approximately 13 hour period where temperatures were below 25F and wind speeds were sufficient to produce wind chill values below 15F. Only approximately 1 hour of this period saw winds at or above 10 mph consistently, while the remainder saw weaker winds. However, the wind chill values produced were cold enough to warrant recording. Temperatures ranged from -6F to 16F, and wind speeds ranged from 6 mph to 13 mph. The coldest wind chill value recorded was -21F. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Deep Creek RAWS station recorded an approximately 1 hour period where temperatures were below 25F and wind chill values were below 15F. Winds were at or above 10 mph, gusting or sustained, through the period. Temperatures hovered around 4F, and winds ranged from 10 mph to 12 mph. The coldest wind chill value recorded during this period was -12F. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front ushered in an arctic airmass on the evening of January 17th, as well as bringing widespread wind and snow to eastern Utah and western Colorado. This arctic airmass was reinforced over the course of the next week by several passing waves with their own associated cold fronts. Temperatures during this period were 15-20 degrees below normal, and in some places close to 30 degrees below normal. With temperatures this cold, any amount of wind would produce a dangerously cold wind chill. The coldest period of this event was January 20th to the 22nd, with temperatures finally beginning to moderate after the 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between the KMTJ ASOS in Montrose and the KAJZ AWOS in Delta, an over 24 hour period of temperatures below 25F and wind chill values below 15F was recorded. Winds were at or above 10 mph for a substantial portion of the period, but even those periods that saw winds weaker than 10 mph saw wind chill values sufficiently cold to warrant being included. Temperatures ranged from -2F to 25F, and winds ranged from 6 mph to 32 mph. The coldest wind chill value recorded was -21F. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low pressure trough moved through the Great Basin and across western Colorado, pulling in remnants of a moderate Atmospheric River that lessened as it moved further inland. However, strong dynamics and instability from the low pressure trough helped overcome the lessening moisture and resulted in light to moderate snow across the central Colorado mountains, with moderate to heavy snowfall over the northern Colorado mountains, especially the Elkhead and Park Mountains, where snowfall amounts exceeded 1 to 2 feet in some areas. Cold air advection and northerly orographic flow on the back side of the trough led to moderate snowfall in the Uncompahgre Gorge of the Northwest San Juan Mountains as well as some moderate snow accumulations in some higher valley locations like Hayden.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 to 10 inches of snow fell across the area with 10 inches of snow reported at Monument. Wind gusts were in the 45 to 52 mph range at the onset of the event at elevations above 11,000 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front associated with a low pressure trough moved northwest to southeast across western Colorado from the Pacific Northwest, resulting in moderate snowfall favoring the northern and central Colorado mountains as well as the Northwest San Juan Mountains.  Heavy snow rates occurred along the cold front with cold air behind it, resulting in higher amounts for some higher valleys including the Upper Yampa River Basin, Hayden, and along Interstate 70 from Glenwood Springs eastward to Vail Pass. Heavier snowfall occurred in the Elkhead and Park Mountains where 1 to 2 feet of snow fell as well as locally higher amounts in the Uncompahgre Gorge of the Northwest San Juan Mountains due to colder air and favored orographic lift.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 12 to 20 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts up to 24 inches at Tower. 12 inches of snow was reported at Steamboat Springs 2.5 N. Winds gusted up to 42 mph at Storm Peak Lab but stayed below 45 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong arctic front and its associated shortwave trough of low pressure moved across western Colorado from north to south, producing light to moderate snowfall across the northern and central Colorado mountains. Locally higher amounts were seen in the Uncompahgre Gorge area of the Northwest San Juan Mountains due to favored northerly orographic lift and efficiently cold temperatures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 8 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts in the Uncompahgre Gorge with 9 inches reported at Monument and 8.2 inches of snow reported at Ouray. Wind gusts were in the 45 to 50 mph range at elevations above 12,000 feet with a peak gust of 50 mph at Telluride Ski Gold Hill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The period between the 11th and 13th of September had a relatively unique meteorological setup for western Colorado and eastern Utah. A potent closed low was located over Southern California, and a ridge of high pressure was located to our southeast, directing a rich plume of monsoonal moisture northward through the Four Corners. Mixing ratios in this plume of moisture were more than high enough to not only help form strong to severe thunderstorms, but also to produce flash flooding and debris flows. Additionally, there was ample instability, with morning clearing helping 600-1000 J/kg of CAPE develop each afternoon. The region also fell under the right exit region of a potent upper level jet streak, which added additional lift and 40-50 kts of shear. Additionally, terrain effects added additional lift and shear. All together, this was a potent setup with the potential to produce severe thunderstorms. And indeed, the storms that developed produced not only large hail and strong, damaging winds, but also two tornadoes in Southeast Utah, and flash flooding across the area. Particularly, fresh burn scars, including the Lee and Elk RBX scars, saw substantial flash flooding and debris flows as storms trained over the area and produced very heavy rain. Finally, after 3 days of widespread severe weather, the cold front pushed through the region, and a cooler, drier, and above all, calmer airmass moved in, bringing an end to the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: National Park Service employees based at Chimney Rock National Monument reported flash flooding on State Route 151 between Pagosa Springs and Arboles. Additionally, washouts and debris were reported in the Chimney Rock National Monument itself, with the LSR reading Upper road and parking lots impassable. Chimney Rock National Monument had to be closed the following day (September 13th, 2025) to clear debris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front associated with a low pressure trough moved northwest to southeast across western Colorado from the Pacific Northwest, resulting in moderate snowfall favoring the northern and central Colorado mountains as well as the Northwest San Juan Mountains.  Heavy snow rates occurred along the cold front with cold air behind it, resulting in higher amounts for some higher valleys including the Upper Yampa River Basin, Hayden, and along Interstate 70 from Glenwood Springs eastward to Vail Pass. Heavier snowfall occurred in the Elkhead and Park Mountains where 1 to 2 feet of snow fell as well as locally higher amounts in the Uncompahgre Gorge of the Northwest San Juan Mountains due to colder air and favored orographic lift.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 6 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts up to 8 inches reported at Glenwood Springs 5.9 S. Winds gusts were in the 25 to 27 mph range throughout Glenwood Canyon.

CZ_NAME: JEFFERSON & W DOUGLAS COUNTIES ABOVE 6000 FEET / GILPIN / CLEAR CREEK / NE PARK COUNTIES BELOW 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread snow fell across the Front Range Mountains and Foothills as well across the urban corridor. Temperatures remained near or above freezing for most of the event below 6000 feet MSL leading to lighter snow accumulation and snow melting on roadways. The strong April sun help keep roads in the mountains and foothills mainly wet during the daylight hours. Roads  became slippery during the nighttime hours in the mountains and foothills. Crashes and safety closures were reported on I-70 and US 6.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reports from various sources showed 5 to 11 inches fell across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The period between the 11th and 13th of September had a relatively unique meteorological setup for western Colorado and eastern Utah. A potent closed low was located over Southern California, and a ridge of high pressure was located to our southeast, directing a rich plume of monsoonal moisture northward through the Four Corners. Mixing ratios in this plume of moisture were more than high enough to not only help form strong to severe thunderstorms, but also to produce flash flooding and debris flows. Additionally, there was ample instability, with morning clearing helping 600-1000 J/kg of CAPE develop each afternoon. The region also fell under the right exit region of a potent upper level jet streak, which added additional lift and 40-50 kts of shear. Additionally, terrain effects added additional lift and shear. All together, this was a potent setup with the potential to produce severe thunderstorms. And indeed, the storms that developed produced not only large hail and strong, damaging winds, but also two tornadoes in Southeast Utah, and flash flooding across the area. Particularly, fresh burn scars, including the Lee and Elk RBX scars, saw substantial flash flooding and debris flows as storms trained over the area and produced very heavy rain. Finally, after 3 days of widespread severe weather, the cold front pushed through the region, and a cooler, drier, and above all, calmer airmass moved in, bringing an end to the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 4:34 PM MDT, the KRIL ASOS at Rifle - Garfield County Regional Airport recorded a gust to 58 mph, with other gusts nearby in the 50-55 mph range. These gusts were associated with a passing thunderstorm. No reports of damages, injuries, or fatalities were received in association with this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved from Arizona to along the New Mexico and southern Colorado border, allowing for light to moderate snowfall across the San Juan Mountains. Outside of the heavier snow in the San Juan Mountains and light snow in the southern valleys, not much snow fell elsewhere.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 9 inches of snow fell across the area. Wind gusts in the 35 to 42 mph range occurred at elevations above 12,000 feet, staying below 45 mph during the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the days after a winter storm that brought accumulating snow to the northwest Colorado valleys, skies cleared and nighttime temperatures dropped off. This allowed trapped low level moisture,  supplemented by the fresh snowfall, to condense into dense fog.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at Craig-Moffat County Airport (KCAG) reported dense fog conditions with visibility under 1/4 for two hours during this period, while the KHDN AWOS at Yampa Valley Airport was in and out of dense fog during this period. No fog was reported at the KEEO ASOS at Meeker Airport. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received with this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong supercell thunderstorm tracked south-southeast across far northeast Weld, southwest Logan and northwest Washington Counties. The storms produced large hail, up to 3 inches in diameter and also three tornadoes across Logan and Washington Counties during the afternoon hours of May 23rd, including one EF-2 and one EF-1. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry and mild December led to the weakening of the snowpack started in November. On top of this now weak snowpack, in the early days of January, a fresh additional 24 inches of snow fell, weakening the snowpack further. This set the stage for potentially injurious or fatal avalanches in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Colorado Avalanche Information Center provided all the information regarding this fatal avalanche accident. The incident occurred in the Northwest San Juan Mountains, on the slopes of Red Mountain #3. The snowpack in this location was known to be weak, and the avalanche danger on the day of the accident was rated as Moderate, or 2/5 on a scale where 1 is the least danger and 5 is the most danger. The victim of the accident, referred to as Skier 1 from here on, was travelling alone although they had arranged a check in system with their spouse. After completing several clean runs, Skier 1 triggered an avalanche that caught and buried them under approximately 1 meter of snow, which prevented self-excavation under the circumstances. When Skier 1 became overdue for the scheduled check-in with their spouse, the spouse notified search and rescue teams, and went to search for their spouse on their own. The spouse found Skier 1 buried and, with the help of nearby backcountry skiers and Ouray Mountain Rescue Team, were able to remove Skier 1 from the avalanche debris. However, Skier 1 had been buried for 4 or more hours, and had unfortunately passed away.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The South Rim Fire started in early July due to multiple lightning strikes 9 miles east of Montrose, Colorado which ignited fires on both the north and south rim of the Black Canyon of the Gunnison National Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The South Rim Fire started in early July due to multiple lightning strikes 9 miles east of Montrose, Colorado which ignited fires on both the north and south rim of the Black Canyon of the Gunnison National Park. The South Rim Fire became the larger of the two, which grew to 4,232 acres before it was contained in early to mid September. The North Rim Fire was quickly put out one day after it started on July 11th. The north rim of the park was reopened to the public July 30th, while the south rim reopened to the public on August 18th. The estimated cost to date was $15,300,000.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Deer Creek Fire started in early July due to human causes 1 mile north of Old La Sal, Utah. The fire grew to 17,724 acres and spread into portions of west-central Colorado particularly western Montrose county, before it was contained in early August. The estimated cost to date was $24,021,755. 11 private structures, a U.S. Forest Service guard station and communication site were destroyed in the fire shortly after it started. A fire induced tornado rated EF2 also occurred on the Deer Creek Fire on July 12, with wind speeds up to 122 mph.

Some oil and gas operations were temporarily shut down as a precaution due to the fire's proximity. The estimated cost to date was $740,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front ushered in an arctic airmass on the evening of January 17th, as well as bringing widespread wind and snow to eastern Utah and western Colorado. This arctic airmass was reinforced over the course of the next week by several passing waves with their own associated cold fronts. Temperatures during this period were 15-20 degrees below normal, and in some places close to 30 degrees below normal. With temperatures this cold, any amount of wind would produce a dangerously cold wind chill. The coldest period of this event was January 20th to the 22nd, with temperatures finally beginning to moderate after the 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The SKYWAY Mesonet station on the Grand Mesa recorded an approximately one hour period where temperatures were below 25F, winds were at or above 10 mph, either gusting or sustained, and wind chill values were below 15F. Temperatures remained around 8F, and winds remained around 10 mph. The coldest wind chill value recorded was -6F. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture from an Atmospheric River off the Pacific was carried by a strong upper level jet, leading to moderate snowfall over portions of the northern and central Colorado mountains, including the Elkhead and Park Mountains as well as the Gore and Elk Mountains.  Strong winds also lead to blowing snow concerns with favored west to northwest flow favoring the northwest Colorado mountain passes for locally higher amounts above a foot.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low pressure trough moved through the Great Basin and across western Colorado, pulling in remnants of a moderate Atmospheric River that lessened as it moved further inland. However, strong dynamics and instability from the low pressure trough helped overcome the lessening moisture and resulted in light to moderate snow across the central Colorado mountains, with moderate to heavy snowfall over the northern Colorado mountains, especially the Elkhead and Park Mountains, where snowfall amounts exceeded 1 to 2 feet in some areas. Cold air advection and northerly orographic flow on the back side of the trough led to moderate snowfall in the Uncompahgre Gorge of the Northwest San Juan Mountains as well as some moderate snow accumulations in some higher valley locations like Hayden.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 7 to 8 inches of snow fell in the Hayden area across the eastern portions of the Central Yampa River Basin with 2 to 4 inches across the western portion of the Central Yampa River Basin, which includes Meeker and Craig. 7.5 inches of snow was reported at Hayden 1.6 SW. Winds gusted in the 25 to 35 mph range with a peak wind gust of 36 mph at Great Divide RAWS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front associated with a low pressure trough moved northwest to southeast across western Colorado from the Pacific Northwest, resulting in moderate snowfall favoring the northern and central Colorado mountains as well as the Northwest San Juan Mountains.  Heavy snow rates occurred along the cold front with cold air behind it, resulting in higher amounts for some higher valleys including the Upper Yampa River Basin, Hayden, and along Interstate 70 from Glenwood Springs eastward to Vail Pass. Heavier snowfall occurred in the Elkhead and Park Mountains where 1 to 2 feet of snow fell as well as locally higher amounts in the Uncompahgre Gorge of the Northwest San Juan Mountains due to colder air and favored orographic lift.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 7 to 8 inches of snow fell across the eastern portions of the Central Yampa River Basin with 8.2 inches of snow reported at Hayden 1.6 SW. 2 to 3 inches of snow fell across the western portions of the Central Yampa River Basin including Craig and Meeker. Wind gusts were in the 25 to 30 mph range with a peak wind gust of 31 mph at Hunter Creek RAWS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep closed low pressure system moved into the Great Basin, bringing a surge of strong Atmospheric River moisture on the order of 200 to 300 percent of normal ahead of it. Some colder air moved in after trough passage as the flow shifted from southwest to northwest, changing rain over to snow in lower elevations. Strong dynamics and high moisture content resulted in widespread moderate to heavy snowfall in most mountain areas of eastern Utah and western Colorado where at least 1 to 2 feet of snow fell with locally higher amounts over 3 feet in some mountain areas. In terms of the lower elevations, the southwest Colorado valleys benefitted the most from the southwest flow with up to a foot or so while other lower valleys saw light to moderate snowfall accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 3 to 6 inches of snow fell across the area with 5.9 inches of snow reported at Rangely 1 E. Wind gusts generally stayed below 25 mph with brief gusts to 25 mph at the onset or just before snow started.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow moved into the mountains during the early morning hours of February 14th. Periods of snowfall continued through the 14th and then ended late on the 15th. The heaviest snowfall occurred over the Park and Gore ranges. An avalanche on Loveland Pass swept a truck off the road and down the slope. No injuries were reported from the avalanche. This led to a long closure of Loveland Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At approximately 7:15PM a natural avalanche released in an avalanche path known as Seven Sister 6, and swept a pickup truck off the road. One person was in the vehicle at the time. They were not seriously injured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backcountry skier is dead after being buried in an avalanche on Berthoud Pass Saturday, according to the Colorado Avalanche Information Center. The skier-triggered avalanche happened on Mines Peak east of the pass, in a rocky part of the mountain face known as The Fingers, around 4:45 p.m. Earlier in the day, a group of people were riding snowmobiles on Gravel Mountain near Grand Lake when they triggered an avalanche. No one was seriously hurt in this earlier slide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably warm temperatures and clear skies promoted deep mixing on the 7th of February. In the meantime, the pressure gradient aloft was tightening ahead of a cold and strong low pressure system approaching the area from the west, leading to strong winds at upper levels. The unusual warmth of the day promoted deep mixing, tapping into those strong upper level winds and bringing strong gusts down to the surface. Higher elevations and the valleys of northeast Utah and northwest Colorado saw the peak gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Carbonate Hill mesonet station recorded a wind gust to 81 mph at 1100 AM MST on February 7th. This was the peak gust of the day, as wind began to diminish during the afternoon in this area. Other gusts around the zone ranged from 45 mph to 60 mph, with the peak gust being this 81 mph gust at Carbonate Hill. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The KEEO ASOS at Meeker Airport in meeker recorded a peak gust to 68 mph at 1143 AM MST on February 7th. Other gusts above 58 mph were recorded at both KEEO ASOS and KHDN AWOS at Yampa Valley Regional Airport in Hayden. At KHDN, two gusts to 58 mph were recorded, one at 602 PM MST and one at 615 PM MST. At KEEO, a gust to 64 mph was recorded at 153 PM MST, a gust to 67 mph was recorded at 357 PM MST, and the peak gust to 68 mph was recorded at 1143 AM MST. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm brought snow to much of eastern Utah and western Colorado. As precipitation began to dwindle over southwestern Colorado, dense fog began to develop in areas that had received fresh snow. These patches of dense fog were isolated and transient, with sensors going in and out of dense fog conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The KDRO ASOS in Durango reported an approximately 2.5 hour period where visibility at the airport was less than a mile, dropping as low as 0.25 mile for a period. This dense fog was confined to the airport, as noted by satellite imagery. Therefore this was an extremely isolated event, making it not a missed Dense Fog Advisory. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a prolonged winter storm from February 14th to the 19th dumped 3-4 feet of snow on the San Juan Mountains, the snow pack in the region was somewhat unstable. The Colorado Avalanche Information Center rated the avalanche danger on February 20th in the area east of Ophir, CO as Moderate or 2/5, and listed the probability of avalanches as possible, with a size of D2, or Small to Large. On this date, in an area known locally as The Nose, two backcountry tourers triggered an avalanche rated as D2.5, big enough to bury, injure, or kill a person,  that caught both and buried and killed one.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Colorado Avalanche Information Center (CAIC) rated the avalanche danger at this location on the day of the incident as Moderate (2/5), with persistent slab avalanches being the main concern. The likelihood of an avalanche was rated as Possible and the expected size was Small to Large (up to D2), large enough to injure, bury, or kill. The period from late November to early February was generally dry, leading to a weak,  low cohesion snowpack onto which 3-4 feet of fresh snow fell from February 14th to the 19th. The incident party was made up of 2 experienced backcountry tourers. Rider 2 was very familiar with the terrain in the area, while this was Skier 1's first time in this area. They had toured together multiple times in other locations prior to this trip. Both read the avalanche forecast on the morning of the incident, and had made a plan to ski lower angle terrain to start, to test the snowpack for any signs of instability. Both had transceivers and carried avalanche probes and shovels. The incident party made for the steeper terrain of a feature known as The Nose in the afternoon. Skier 1 started his descent and within 5-10 second triggered an avalanche that caught him, but he was able to escape the avalanche 150-200 vertical feet below his starting point. Once out of the avalanche he looked and called for Rider 2 and received no response. Skier 1 began a transceiver search and, after about an hour of searching the widely spread out avalanche debris, finally located Rider 2, buried under about 6 feet of snow. An additional pair of rescuers had noticed the fresh avalanche and arrived in the area, searching on their own before finding Skier 1 and helping dig out Rider 2. When found, Rider 2 was not breathing and had no pulse. Rider 2 was finally fully excavated about 90 minutes after the avalanche, and while Silverton Medical Rescue arrived and provided care, even airlifting them to the hospital in Silverton, Rider 2 was pronounced dead.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm brought snow to much of eastern Utah and western Colorado. As precipitation began to dwindle over southwestern Colorado, dense fog began to develop in areas that had received fresh snow. These patches of dense fog were isolated and transient, with sensors going in and out of dense fog conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The KGUC AWOS in Gunnison recorded an approximately 7 hour period where visibilities dropped as low as 0.25 mile. This was in between periods of light to moderate snowfall. The fog during this period was transient, with periods of visibility as low as 0.25 mile and periods with visibility of 10 miles or better. Therefore, due to the transient nature of this dense fog, this was not a missed Dense Fog event. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a prolonged winter storm from February 14th to the 19th dumped 3-4 feet of snow on the San Juan Mountains, the snow pack in the region was somewhat unstable. The Colorado Avalanche Information Center rated the avalanche danger on February 25th in the area of Ophir, CO as Moderate or 2/5, and listed the probability of avalanches as possible, with a size of D3, or Large to Very Large. On this date, in an area known locally as Mine Dump, a backcountry tourer triggered an avalanche rated as D1.5, big enough to injure but not necessarily to kill. This skier was caught and carried into a tree, which did cause injuries and required the aid of San Miguel County Search and rescue for excavation and evacuation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Colorado Avalanche Information Center rated the avalanche danger at this location on the day of the incident as Moderate (2/5), with persistent slab avalanches being the main concern. The likelihood of an avalanche was rated as Possible and the expected size was Large to Very Large (up to D3), large enough to not only bury, injure, or kill a person but also to bury cars, destroy a house, or break trees. The period from late November through early February had been generally dry, punctuated by strong but brief winter storms. In the week prior to this event, a major winter storm had brough a fresh 3-4 feet of snow to the area, along with light accumulations on subsequent days. This led to a weak, unstable snowpack that had already resulted in two fatal avalanche accidents in the days prior. On the day of the accident, two backcountry skiers had opted to ski an area know locally as Mine Dump, a steep, north-facing slope west of the town of Ophir, CO. Skier 1 triggered an avalanche in the upper snowpack that then stepped down to deep weak layers twice before reaching the ground. This avalanche caught skier one and carried him into a tree, injuring him. Skier 2, who was not caught, alerted a friend via radio, and the friend alerted San Miguel County Search and Rescue. San Miguel County Search and Rescue freed Skier 1 and evacuated him to a waiting ambulance, which later transferred him to a helicopter to airlift him to hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level jet passed over the region on February 5th. Deep mixing, thanks to unseasonably warm temperatures, was able to tap into the 130-140 knot jet aloft and bring down some stronger wind gusts. Most of the area generally saw gusts in the 35-45 mph range, but a few gusts in excess of 60 mph were recorded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Colorado Department of Transportation Mesonet site 082w023 Basalt-Holland Hills recorded a peak wind gust to 83 mph at 2:51 PM MST on February 5th. Other gusts across the zone ranged from 45 mph to 75 mph. Winds had picked up during the early hours of the morning, peaking in the early afternoon before finally diminishing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the wake of a winter storm that brought fresh snow to the central Yampa River Valley, pockets of dense fog developed early on the morning of February 18th. These pockets were generally isolated and transient.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The KCAG ASOS in Craig, CO recorded an approximately 5 hour period with visibility dropping as low as 1/4 mile, and climbing as high as 10 miles. For the majority of this period, visibility was less than 1 mile, but the transient nature of the fog allowed for those periods of significant improvement. Due to the isolated nature of this fog event, this was not a missed Dense Fog Advisory. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the wake of a storm that brought fresh snow to the Northwest Colorado Valleys, skies began to clear. As temperatures dropped, dense fog began to develop in and around Meeker.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The KEEO ASOS at Meeker Airport recorded an approximately 1 hour period where visibility was reduced due to fog. Visibilities were a quarter mile or less for only about 20 minutes, but were below 1 mile for the full period. That said, this dense fog was localized to the Meeker area, and therefore this was not a missed Dense Fog Advisory. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong Atmospheric River moisture on the heels of a strong upper level jet moved across northwest Colorado, with moderate to heavy snowfall over the Elkhead and Park Mountains with lighter snowfall amounts elsewhere. Winds were strong with this system as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 8 to 15 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts up to 20 inches at Tower. Wind gusts were in the 53 to 60 mph range at Storm Peak Lab.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough and associated cold front moved across northwest Colorado, bringing moderate snowfall to the Elkhead and Park Mountains with lighter snow accumulations elsewhere. Enough cold air moved in across the north to allow for moderate snow across the eastern portions of the Central Yampa River Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 3 to 7 inches of snow fell across the area with 3 to 5 inches on the west end at Craig and Maybell, and higher amounts of 6 to 7 inches on the east end around Hayden. 6.7 inches of snow was reported at the Hayden COOP. Winds gusted in the 25 to 50 mph range with a peak wind gust of 54 mph at the Craig-Moffat County Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A positively tilted trough and cold front moved swiftly across northwest Colorado, bringing very cold air and light to moderate snow across the northern and central Colorado mountains, with heavier accumulations favoring the Gore and Elk mountains. Strong winds also accompanied this quick moving system, leading to blowing snow impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 8 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts up to 10 to 14 inches at Beaver Creek and McCoy Park. Wind gusts were in the 45 to 58 mph range at elevations above 12,000 feet with a peak wind gust of 73 mph at Snowmass Ski Area Baldy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure trough followed on the heels of the strong moisture laden system around Valentines Day, with remnants of a moderate Atmospheric River and colder air, bringing moderate to heavy snowfall across the northern and central Colorado mountains where another one to two feet of snow fell. The northwest Colorado valleys and the Uncompahgre Gorge of the northwest San Juans also received moderate snowfall accumulations due to favored orographics and sufficiently colder air.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 10 inches fell across the area with 10.5 inches at the Hayden COOP. Wind gusts were in the 25 to 35 mph range with a peak gust of 38 mph at the Pinto RAWS.

The month of August continued the trend of the summer, where above normal temperatures and below normal precipitation were characteristic. By the latter part of the month, Extreme (D3) Drought conditions expanded into the higher elevations of the San Juan mountains, as well as into the southern Colorado valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The August 20th US Drought Monitor update showed the worsening of drought conditions across the southern Colorado valleys, with Colorado Zone 023 - San Juan River Basin developing Extreme (D3) Drought conditions across roughly 2/3 of the zone. The extent of this drought remained unchanged through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A jet streak riding through the northwesterly flow aloft brough some high synoptic winds to parts of northeast Utah and along the higher elevations of the Colorado Divide. In addition, upper level moisture riding that jet led to the development of some showers across the terrain, but dry surface levels led to gusty outflows, which enhanced the already gusty winds in spots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Colorado Department of Transportation mesonet site 082w023 Basalt-Holland Hills recorded a wind gust to 76 mph at approximately 6:00 PM MDT. This was the only gust above threshold recorded, and was likely due to enhancement from non-convective precipitation nearby. No reports of injuries, fatalities, or damages were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With enough moisture present in the wake of a departing low pressure system, and clear skies allowing for overnight lows to cool close to the dew point, areas of dense fog developed across the Grand Valley. In spots, particularly in the Redlands area of southern Grand Junction, dense fog developed that had visibility of less than 150 yards.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This localized Dense Fog Event was reported to NWS Grand Junction by a former employee of this office. Also included were photographs indicating that yes, visibility was less than 1/4 mile in this area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clear skies and light winds in the wake of a low pressure system allowed temperatures to drop below freezing for many of the lower elevation valleys of southwestern Colorado. The growing season had been ongoing for around a month, with few freeze events prior to this. Some locations saw temperatures at or below 28F for at least 3 hours, marking this as a hard freeze event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The coldest temperature recorded at the KDRO ASOS in Durango, CO was 28F, while the coldest temperature recorded at any recording site in the region was 24F. Temperatures were at or below 32F for an average of 3.6 hours, with 11 of the 16 available recording sites seeing temperatures at or below 32F for any length of time. The average time of temperatures at or below 28F was only 1.2 hours, meaning this was not a hard freeze event.  No reports of crop losses or damages were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong supercell thunderstorm tracked south-southeast across far northeast Weld, southwest Logan and northwest Washington Counties. The storms produced large hail, up to 3 inches in diameter and also three tornadoes across Logan and Washington Counties during the afternoon hours of May 23rd, including one EF-2 and one EF-1. Most of the observed damage was snapped power poles and trees. One single family residence sustained EF-1 damage, but there were no injuries reported. This supercell also produced a swath of golf ball to baseball sized hail, with one ICECHIP report of over 3 inch diameter hail in northern Washington county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report from Mping.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clear skies and light winds in the wake of a low pressure system allowed temperatures to drop below freezing for many of the lower elevation valleys of southwestern Colorado. The growing season had been ongoing for around a month, with few freeze events prior to this. Some locations saw temperatures at or below 28F for at least 3 hours, marking this as a hard freeze event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There is no ASOS or AWOS available in this zone. The coldest temperature recorded at any RAWS site was 24F, at the San Juan Portable RAWS #2. The coldest temperature recorded at any site was 22F. Temperatures were at or below 32F for an average of 4.8 hours, with 5 of the 7 available sites seeing temperatures below 32F for any duration. The average time that temperatures at or below 28F were recorded was 3.5 hours, which marks this as a hard freeze event. No reports of crop losses or damages were recorded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another low pressure system with an associated cold front moved through the region in the days prior to this freeze event. The night of the event, clouds cleared and clear skies prevailed, along with generally light winds. Both of these promoted good radiational cooling, which combined with some modest cold air advection to drop temperatures below freezing in areas where vegetation was sensitive.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The coldest temperature recorded at the KDRO ASOS at the Durango-La Plata County Airport was 23F, while the coldest temperature recorded at any site was 22F. Thirteen of the seventeen available sensors recorded at least one hour with temperatures at or below 32F, with an average duration of 4.3 hours. The average duration of temperatures at or below 28F was only 1.7 hours, marking this as not being a hard freeze event. No reports of crop losses or damages were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another low pressure system with an associated cold front moved through the region in the days prior to this freeze event. The night of the event, clouds cleared and clear skies prevailed, along with generally light winds. Both of these promoted good radiational cooling, which combined with some modest cold air advection to drop temperatures below freezing in areas where vegetation was sensitive.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There is no available ASOS or AWOS site. The coldest temperature recorded at a RAWS site in the zone was 23F at the San Juan Portable RAWS #2, while the coldest temperature recorded at any sensor in the zone was 21F. Seven of the eight available sensors recorded temperatures at or below 32F for at least an hour, and the average duration of temperatures at or below 32F was 6.8 hours. Additionally, temperatures at or below 28F lasted for an average of 3.8 hours, marking this as a hard freeze event. No reports of crop losses or damages were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasing upper level moisture across eastern Utah and western Colorado led to the development of daily afternoon showers and thunderstorms over the higher terrain. However, dry surface levels meant that all precipitation falling out of this activity evaporated before reaching the ground, enhancing the convective outflow winds and allowing for stronger than expected gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Colorado Department of Transportation mesonet station 070w001 Utah State Line recorded a wind gust to 62 mph at 5:06 PM MDT, associated with a thunderstorm moving off the higher terrain to the south over the Uncompahgre Plateau. No reports of damages, injuries, or fatalities were received.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Colorado Department of Transportation mesonet site 082w023 Basalt-Holland Hills recorded a wind gust to 75 mph at 1:58 PM MDT. This gust was associated with ongoing afternoon thunderstorms over the adjacent higher terrain. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

CZ_NAME: FOUR CORNERS / UPPER DOLORES RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As warm and dry weather persisted across eastern Utah and western Colorado during the month of May, Extreme (D3) Drought conditions expanded into some of the lower elevations of southwest Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The US Drought Monitor expanded the area of Extreme (D3) Drought into the northernmost portion of the Four Corners/Upper Dolores River Basin (CO Zone 021). This area covers less than 5% of the zone, and remained unchanged from this point to the end of the month.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After seeing a below normal snowpack in the winter of 2024-2025, and after a hot and dry month of May, Extreme (D3) Drought expanded into portions of the Colorado Central Divide Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Beginning on May 27th, the US Drought Monitor expanded the area of Extreme (D3) Drought conditions into the Gore and Elk Mountains and Central Mountain Valleys (CO Zone 010). This area of Extreme (D3) Conditions covered less than 5% of the zone and was generally in an area of higher terrain. This area remained unchanged from the 27th through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong supercell thunderstorm tracked south-southeast across far northeast Weld, southwest Logan and northwest Washington Counties. The storms produced large hail, up to 3 inches in diameter and also three tornadoes across Logan and Washington Counties during the afternoon hours of May 23rd, including one EF-2 and one EF-1. Most of the observed damage was snapped power poles and trees. One single family residence sustained EF-1 damage, but there were no injuries reported. This supercell also produced a swath of golf ball to baseball sized hail, with one ICECHIP report of over 3 inch diameter hail in northern Washington county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The formation of this tornado was captured well by storm chaser video. The tornado did not have a coherent damage path. There was sporadic damage south of the South Platte River, primarily to a trio of grain silos. Two silos had their tops removed, one was shifted off the pad, another was destroyed. One off duty NWS employee confirmed the location of the tornado as it crossed Logan County Road R just south of County Road 59. The tornado dissipated just southwest of the Prewitt Reservoir with no evidence it crossed I-76.
