Most of the observed damage was snapped power poles and trees. One single family residence sustained EF-1 damage, but there were no injuries reported. This supercell also produced a swath of golf ball to baseball sized hail, with one ICECHIP report of over 3 inch diameter hail in northern Washington county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Well documented tornado that moved south parallel to CO-63, remaining generally in open areas 2-3 miles west of CO-63. We obtained the width (400 yds) of the tornado based on power poles snapped along County Road 50. One single family residence was struck with minor damage to the house, and significant damage to all outbuildings and trees on the property, consistent with EF-1 damage. Debris from the property was carried approximately one mile to the south-southwest. Further south of the damaged property, power poles were snapped and thrown a considerable distance (25 yds), consistent with EF-2 intensity. The power poles were on the west side of AA, just north of County Road 48. Video evidence and the results of the storm survey, suggest that the tornado dissipated just before 6 PM MDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Elk RBX Fire started in early August due to a lightning strike 11 miles southeast of Meeker, Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Elk RBX Fire started in early August due to a lightning strike 11 miles southeast of Meeker, Colorado. The fire grew to 14,518 acres before it was contained in mid August. The fire destroyed 4 residential homes and one outbuilding. The estimated cost to date was $10,300,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Grease Fire started in early August due to a lightning strike 12 miles west-southwest of Meeker, Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Grease Fire started in early August due to a lightning strike 12 miles west-southwest of Meeker, Colorado. The fire grew to 1,000 acres before merging into the Lee Fire and acreage was combined for both fires one day later, in which the complex was then just known as the Lee Fire. The end date and time is the moment when the Grease was combined into the Lee Fire. See Lee Fire episode for further information.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Lee Fire started in early August due to a lightning strike 20 miles southwest of Meeker, Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lee Fire started in early August due to a lightning strike 20 miles southwest of Meeker, Colorado. The Grease Fire grew into the Lee Fire on August 4 and acreage was combined and further known as the Lee Fire. The fire grew to 137,758 acres before it was contained in mid September. The estimated cost to date was $30,106,091. At least five homes and 14 additional structures, such as outbuildings were destroyed by the Lee Fire. The Lee Fire became the fifth largest wildfire in Colorado history. Despite the damage, no injuries or deaths were reported.

CZ_NAME: FOUR CORNERS / UPPER DOLORES RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The month of September ended on a generally dry note, particularly for lower elevation area. While the month of September had been moist enough for most locations to see drought improvement through the month, the Four Corners region saw an opposite trend. Drought in this region actually worsened during the last quarter of the month, with the expansion of Extreme (D3) Drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 9/24 US Drought Monitor update analyzed an area of Extreme (D3) Drought that had developed in Colorado Zone 021 - Four Corners/Upper Dolores River Basin. The majority of the zone remained under Severe (D2) Drought conditions, but an area of Extreme (D3) Drought now covered roughly 10% of the zone. These conditions remained largely unchanged through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The period between the 11th and 13th of September had a relatively unique meteorological setup for western Colorado and eastern Utah. A potent closed low was located over Southern California, and a ridge of high pressure was located to our southeast, directing a rich plume of monsoonal moisture northward through the Four Corners. Mixing ratios in this plume of moisture were more than high enough to not only help form strong to severe thunderstorms, but also to produce flash flooding and debris flows. Additionally, there was ample instability, with morning clearing helping 600-1000 J/kg of CAPE develop each afternoon. The region also fell under the right exit region of a potent upper level jet streak, which added additional lift and 40-50 kts of shear. Additionally, terrain effects added additional lift and shear. All together, this was a potent setup with the potential to produce severe thunderstorms. And indeed, the storms that developed produced not only large hail and strong, damaging winds, but also two tornadoes in Southeast Utah, and flash flooding across the area. Particularly, fresh burn scars, including the Lee and Elk RBX scars, saw substantial flash flooding and debris flows as storms trained over the area and produced very heavy rain. Finally, after 3 days of widespread severe weather, the cold front pushed through the region, and a cooler, drier, and above all, calmer airmass moved in, bringing an end to the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At approximately 4:48 PM MDT, the KEEO ASOS at Meeker Airport recorded a gust to 58 mph in association with a nearby thunderstorm. Other gusts in the area ranged from 45 mph to 55 mph. No reports of damages, fatalities, or injuries were received in association with this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A plume of anomalously rich monsoonal moisture pushed into eastern Utah and western Colorado in the days prior to these debris flows. On the day of the flows, precipitable water values were in excess of 1.00 inch across the region. Daytime heating acting on the complex terrain of the region allowed for the formation of thunderstorms during the afternoon. These storms were capable of producing heavy rainfall with very high rain rates. Over the course of the day, multiple of these storms moved over the recent Lee and Elk RBX burn scars in Rio Blanco county, triggering multiple debris flows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This incident was reported to NWS Grand Junction by the NWS Incident Meteorologist deployed to the Lee Wildfire. Water was reported as flowing over Highway 13 adjacent to the Lee Burn Scar, near the incident on County Road 5, and south of the town of Meeker. All of this water drains into the White River, and the gage on the White River below Meeker spiked from roughly 110 cfs to 1660 cfs, with a rise in water height of 3 feet.

No direct measurement of the winds was recorded, therefore the wind for this entry was estimated. This was the only reported damage, and there were no reports of injuries or fatalities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unusually rich monsoonal moisture remained in place over eastern Utah and western Colorado on the 28th. With daytime heating over the complex terrain providing lift, showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and tapped into this moisture, producing heavy rain and high rain rates. These storms were capable of producing flooding and debris flows over sensitive terrain, including slot canyons and debris flows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NWS Incident Meteorologist deployed to the Lee Fire reported this event to NWS Grand Junction. Thunderstorms produced heavy rain over the Lee Burn Scar on the afternoon of August 28th. This in turn resulted in water, mud, and debris flowing over Rio Blanco County Road 5 near mile marker 30, roughly 20 miles west of meeker and on the west side of the Lee Burn Scar. A Flash Flood Warning had been issued for this region with 30 minutes of lead time. No reports of damages, injuries, or fatalities were received. County Road 5 did have to be closed for a short duration to clear the debris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Coulter Creek Fire started in early August due to an undetermined cause 4 miles northeast of Carbondale, Colorado. However, lightning in the days prior was suspected to be a factor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Coulter Creek Fire started in early August due to an undetermined cause 4 miles northeast of Carbondale, Colorado. However, lightning in the days prior was suspected to be a factor. The fire grew to 107 acres before it was contained one day later in early August.  No homes were lost, but one outbuilding was destroyed.

Road did need to be cleared, but was already closed to public traffic due to the ongoing Elk RBX fire. No additional damage, or injuries or fatalities were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge of high pressure remained overhead with moisture gradually increasing underneath the high pressure, resulting in hot temperatures and daily afternoon showers and thunderstorms. With the dry low layers, strong gusty outflow winds were the main concern with one particular thunderstorm producing a peak wind gust of 58 mph at the Rifle Garfield County Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm over the Rifle Garfield County Airport produced a peak wind gust of 58 mph, measured at 1631 MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The month of August continued the trend of the summer, where above normal temperatures and below normal precipitation were characteristic. By the latter part of the month, Extreme (D3) Drought conditions expanded into the higher elevations of the San Juan mountains, as well as into the southern Colorado valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With August 20th update of the US Drought Monitor, about 3/4 of Colorado Zone 022 - Animas River Basin had seen drought conditions worsen to Extreme (D3) Drought. The extent of this drought remained largely unchanged through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed low pressure system moved across the Four Corners into southwest Colorado, bringing abundant moisture and enough cold air to result in moderate to heavy snowfall across the San Juan Mountains, especially above timberline, where snowfall exceeded a foot. Very little if any snow fell elsewhere due to the concentration of dynamics with the low, with rain primarily below 9000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 7 inches of snow fell across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A negatively tilted low pressure trough and associated cold front moved through eastern Utah and western Colorado, bringing much colder air and gusty winds in addition to light to moderate snowfall in the mountains. Given the late season nature of this event in mid May, roads remained slushy and snow packed at times at the higher passes causing some travel impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 3 to 6 inches of snow fell across the area with peak wind gust of 42 mph at Storm Peak, so stayed below 45 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moving into the Pacific Northwest led to the upper level pressure gradient to begin tightening over the eastern Great Basin, leading to increasing winds aloft. At the same time, high pressure still in place over eastern Utah and western Colorado helped promote deep daytime mixing that allowed those strong upper level winds to mix down to the surface. This resulted in widespread wind gusts of 40-50 mph, with gusts upwards of 60 mph in some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: All the gusts above the 75 mph threshold were recorded at the Colorado Department of Transportation Douglas Pass mesonet station. The peak gust to 83 mph was recorded at 2:29 PM MDT, while another gust to 78 mph occurred at 5:12 PM MDT. Other sites across the zone only saw gusts into the 40s and 50s. However, geography and elevation contribute to this site seeing such high winds. Douglas Pass is a gap oriented north to south with higher terrain to the east and west of the gap. This would act to funnel winds through, with strongest gusts occurring when winds are coming from the south or the north. Winds on this day were southerly, maximizing the funneling effect. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure trough tracked across the Great Basin with strong dynamics and instability ahead of it, resulting in moderate snowfall favoring the southern and central Colorado mountains with lighter amounts across the north. This system was fairly mild which limited snowfall amounts in the lower valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 7 to 13 inches of snow fell across the area with 13 inches reported at Vail 2.1 ENE. Wind gusts were in the 40 to 55 mph range at elevations above 12,000 feet with a peak wind gust of 57 mph at Snowmass Ski Area Baldy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure trough and associated cold front brought mild air ahead of it and much colder air behind it to result in light to moderate snowfall across most mountain areas of eastern Utah and western Colorado with rain changing over to snow in many lower valley locations even though accumulations remained light. Heavier snowfall seemed to favor the La Sal Mountains of southeast Utah as well as the northwest San Juans and Grand Mesa mainly due to the colder northerly flow behind the cold front. Winds were also fairly strong leading to blowing and drifting snow in some mountain areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 12 to 20 inches of snow fell across the area with 20 inches reported at Skyway. Peak wind gust of 47 mph occurred at Mesa at the onset of the event, however the report out of Skyway indicated that the snow was wind affected.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper trough passed through the region near the middle of March. This allowed for sufficient lift to drop some quick, heavy mountain snow out west. Meanwhile, strong winds descended the mountain slopes and spread across the eastern plains. Widespread blowing dust impacted visibility close to the Colorado-Kansas border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a 64 mph wind gust measured.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was an 87 mph wind gust measured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure trough tracked across the Great Basin with strong dynamics and instability ahead of it, resulting in moderate snowfall favoring the southern and central Colorado mountains with lighter amounts across the north. This system was fairly mild which limited snowfall amounts in the lower valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 to 12 inches of snow fell across the area with 7 inches reported at McClure Pass. Wind gusts were in the 45 to 55 mph range at elevations above 11,900 feet with a peak wind gust of 59 mph at Cinnamon Mountain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper trough passed through the region near the middle of March. This allowed for sufficient lift to drop some quick, heavy mountain snow out west. EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a 62 mph wind gust measured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep closed low pressure system moved through the Great Basin into the Four Corners region on the heels of another system. This low brought deep Atmospheric River moisture from the Desert Southwest through the Four Corners ahead of it, along with impressive dynamics and a strong jet to produce moderate to heavy snowfall across most mountain ranges of eastern Utah and western Colorado where the highest amounts of 1 to 2 feet of snow fell across the mountains of eastern Utah and southwest Colorado. Temperatures remained mild enough that most lower valleys saw primarily rain, limiting snowfall accumulations.  Winds were quite strong as well with gusts in the mountains in the 60 to 80 mph range on average, leading to impacts from blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 8 to 15 inches of snow fell across the area with up to 19 inches at Schofield Pass. Winds generally gusted in the 50 to 80 mph range at elevations above 11,900 feet with a peak wind gust of 87 mph at Cinnamon Mountain on March 6 at 19:30.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving cold front and shortwave trough moved through eastern Utah and western Colorado, bringing moderate snowfall across most mountain areas of eastern Utah and western Colorado. The higher amounts fell across the mountains of southeast Utah and southwest Colorado due to favorable orographics and better available moisture. In addition, strong winds led to blowing snow across the mountain areas. Light to moderate snowfall fell across some valley locations including portions of the southwest Colorado valley foothills as well as central mountain corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 2 to 5 inches of snow fell across much of the area with locally higher amounts of 7 to 10 inches of snow at locations south of Glenwood Springs and north of Carbondale along Highway 82. 10.2 inches of snow was reported at Glenwood Springs 7.2 S. Winds generally gusted in the 25 to 35 mph range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A generally warm and dry month of May in combination with a drier than normal winter has led to Extreme (D3) Drought to persist across portions of western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought remained unchanged across the Grand Valley, with only the eastern half of the zone under Extreme (D3) Drought conditions during the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A generally warm and dry month of May in combination with a drier than normal winter has led to Extreme (D3) Drought to persist across portions of western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The area of Extreme (D3) Drought along the spine of the far southern Divide mountains in western Colorado remained unchanged during the month of May.

CZ_NAME: CENTRAL GUNNISON AND UNCOMPAHGRE RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A generally warm and dry month of May in combination with a drier than normal winter has led to Extreme (D3) Drought to persist across portions of western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Central Gunnison and Uncompahgre River Basin (CO Zone 011) started the month of May with Extreme (D3) Drought analyzed across over 75% of the zone. By the end of the month, this area of Extreme (D3) Drought had expanded to cover over 90% of the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A generally warm and dry month of May in combination with a drier than normal winter has led to Extreme (D3) Drought to persist across portions of western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the beginning of the month of May, Extreme (D3) Drought was analyzed across the furthest western tip of the Upper Gunnison River Valley (CO Zone 014), covering less than 10% of the zone. By the end of the month, that area of Extreme (D3) Drought had expanded slightly, covering approximately 10% of the zone. However the area under Moderate (D1) and Severe (D2) Drought had expanded greatly by the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A generally warm and dry month of May in combination with a drier than normal winter has led to Extreme (D3) Drought to persist across portions of western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Uncompahgre Plateau and Dallas Divide (CO Zone 017) began the month with around 50% of the zone under Extreme (D3) Drought conditions. By the end of the month, the area analyzed as being under Extreme (D3) Drought conditions had expanded to cover around 75% of the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A generally warm and dry month of May in combination with a drier than normal winter has led to Extreme (D3) Drought to persist across portions of western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The De Beque to Silt Corridor (CO Zone 007) began the month with Extreme (D3) Drought at its western extent, covering less than a quarter of the zone. This area expanded slightly over the course of the month, but remained less than a quarter of the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A generally warm and dry month of May in combination with a drier than normal winter has led to Extreme (D3) Drought to persist across portions of western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A very small portion of the Roan and Tavaput Plateau zone is under Extreme (D3) Drought conditions, but these conditions persisted throughout the month of May. In addition, the area of Severe (D2) Drought expanded substantially across the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A generally warm and dry month of May in combination with a drier than normal winter has led to Extreme (D3) Drought to persist across portions of western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) Drought was analyzed as covering over half of the Grand and Battlement Mesas zone at the beginning of the month of May. By the end of the month, Extreme (D3) Drought had expanded to cover over 80% of the zone, with only the far northern portion of the zone still under Severe (D2) conditions.

CZ_NAME: PARADOX VALLEY / LOWER DOLORES RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As warm and dry weather persisted across eastern Utah and western Colorado during the month of May, Extreme (D3) Drought conditions expanded into some of the lower elevations of southwest Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The US Drought Monitor expanded Extreme (D3) Drought into the Paradox Valley/Lower Dolores River Basin (CO Zone 020) starting on May 13th. This area began by covering roughly the central third of the zone, but by the end of the month had expanded to cover the western half of the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms brought strong winds to southeast Weld and Morgan Counties during the evening of June 14th with gusts as high as 73 mph.

Strong to severe thunderstorms tracked eastward bringing strong wind gusts and hail up to one inch on diameter during the afternoon of June 24th.
Broad southwesterly flow helped initiate an isolated supercell across the far eastern plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was 1.5 inch hail measured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the wake of a strong spring storm that pushed a potent cold front through the area, clear skies and calm winds allowed temperatures to drop below freezing across much of the Grand Valley. With several periods of much above normal temperatures punctuated by near-normal temperatures, the growing season in the area was well underway. Therefore a widespread freeze at this time of the year could have a detrimental effect on fruit crops and other agriculture in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The minimum temperature recorded at the KGJT ASOS at the Grand Junction Regional Airport was 25F, while the minimum temperature recorded at any observation site was 10F. 19 of the 20 surface observation stations in the zone recorded temperatures at or below 32F for at least 3 hours, with the average being 10 hours, the maximum being 18 hours, and the minimum being 2 hours. Additionally, this event qualified as a hard freeze, as the sensors recorded an average of 6.5 hours at or below 28F, with a maximum of 18 hours and a minimum of 0. No reports of crop damages or losses were received with this event, likely owing to the early season nature of it.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong to severe thunderstorms developed/moved east into East Central Colorado during the early evening.  The main threat with these storms was straight-line wind gusts, with the highest reported gust being 67 MPH reported north of Clarkville.  Some of the thunderstorms produced hail, with the largest reported being quarter size near Wauneta.  As the storms moved toward the east border, they produced an outflow boundary from the straight-line winds.  This boundary began to produce blowing dust before exiting Colorado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually dry winter and underperforming snowpack combined with a lack of precipitation across the lower elevations during the spring months to reintroduce drought to Western Colorado. By late April, the first widespread drought conditions of Extreme (D3) or greater category appeared.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A sliver of Extreme (D3) drought conditions appeared in far eastern Archuleta County, along the southern San Juan Mountains. This amounted to less than 0.5% of the entire CWA. This area of D3 remained unchanged through the month, with Moderate (D1) and Severe (D2) conditions filling in the remainder of the zone.

CZ_NAME: CENTRAL GUNNISON AND UNCOMPAHGRE RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually dry winter and underperforming snowpack combined with a lack of precipitation across the lower elevations during the spring months to reintroduce drought to Western Colorado. By late April, the first widespread drought conditions of Extreme (D3) or greater category appeared.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The US Drought Monitor update on April 29th added a large pocket of Extreme (D3) Drought to central Western Colorado, including portions of Mesa, Delta, and Montrose Counties. This area, combined with the small sliver of D3 conditions in the southern San Juans, put just under 6.5% of the entire County Warning Area under Extreme Drought conditions. Severe (D2) Drought conditions make up the remainder of this zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The thunderstorms produced a brief landspout tornado northwest of Denver International Airport. As the storms progressed eastward, hail up to one inch in diameter and wind gusts up to 64 mph were reported.

Strong to severe thunderstorms formed along the I-25 Corridor north of Denver during the early morning hours of June 17th. The main severe weather type was large hail with the largest being 2.75 inches in diameter. These storms tracked southeast across northeast Colorado during the early morning hours and exited the area around 0700 MST. Additional strong to severe thunderstorms redeveloped during the early afternoon hours along the I-25 corridor and then tracked eastward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report from mping.

These storms tracked southeast across northeast Colorado during the early morning hours and exited the area around 0700 MST. Additional strong to severe thunderstorms redeveloped during the early afternoon hours along the I-25 corridor and then tracked eastward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report from mping.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong to severe thunderstorms developed/moved east into East Central Colorado during the early evening.  The main threat with these storms was straight-line wind gusts, with the highest reported gust being 67 MPH reported north of Clarkville.  Some of the thunderstorms produced hail, with the largest reported being quarter size near Wauneta.  As the storms moved toward the east border, they produced an outflow boundary from the straight-line winds.  This boundary began to produce blowing dust before exiting Colorado.

In the days prior to this avalanche incident, the area in question had received a few inches of fresh snowfall from a spring storm. Additionally, some heightened winds had led to the development of what is know as wind slabs at the higher elevations in this range. While avalanche danger wasn't rated excessively high (at a 2 out of 5, with 5 being the maximum), there was still enough of a threat that any one avalanche could injure or kill a backcountry recreator.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Per the CAIC forecast for the day of the incident, the overall avalanche threat in the area of the incident was rated as Moderate (2/5), with Low (1/5) being the lowest threat and Extreme (5/5) being the highest. The breakdown at different elevation bands gave Moderate (2/5) threats Near and Above treeline, while areas below treeline were rated as Low (1/5). The main avalanche type of concern was Wind Slab, with west through north through to east facing slopes the areas of concern. Likelihood was rated as Possible (2/5), with Unlikely (1/5) the lowest likelihood and Certain (5/5) the highest. The expected size was rated as Small to Large, meaning avalanches could range from mostly harmless outside of unfavorable terrain to large enough to bury, injure, or kill someone. The Avalanche Forecast Discussion for this area highlighted the fact that the avalanche problem was isolated, but reactive when found. You won't find many dangerous wind-loaded slopes, but when you do, there is a good chance you will trigger an avalanche. As for the actual incident avalanche, little data is available from the summary. The incident occurred on an east-facing slope at treeline, where the avalanche threat was rated as Moderate (2/5). Two backcountry skiers were in the area, and had already noticed some sloughing in the snowpack but as it was very unconsolidated and light, had decided to continue to ski. At the top of their second lap down, the slough had consolidated and was moving much faster, catching both skiers on their way down. Skier 1, who wrote the report, was able to escape, but Skier 2 lost a ski in the avalanche was carried all the way down, and ultimately was injured. No information was given as to the severity of the injury. The avalanche was rated as D1.5, or the higher end of a Small avalanche, and was small relative to its path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts up to 59 MPH were reported in Yuma County with a group of thunderstorms moving southeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ambient weather site Struckmeyer reported the gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms with strong damaging winds tracked across northeast Colorado during the afternoon and evening of May 14th. High winds of 60-80 mph reported in numerous locations, and at least a couple reports of trees that fell onto homes. One in Brush, CO, and two more homes had damage due to fallen trees in Holyoke, CO. Power outages were reported in Holyoke, Iliff, Brush, and some surrounding rural locations in northeast Colorado. Strong winds from a cold front (enhanced from the thunderstorm outflow winds) pushed through eastern Elbert and Lincoln Counties with gusts of 55 to 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Located at the Akron airport AKO.

Anomalously deep moisture was present across southeast Utah and southwest Colorado on the afternoon of June 4th. Enough sunshine had gotten through during the morning hours to create afternoon instability of 500-1000 J/Kg, more than enough to produce some strong storms. In addition, atmospheric profiles favored heavy rain development and slow moving, training thunderstorms. All these factors combined to lead to a few instances of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was observed along Salt Creek down to the confluence with the Dolores River, near the intersection of Salt Creek Road and Colorado Highway 141. A COOP observer nearby reported a pickup truck caught in the flood and floated upright downstream. Additionally, a Colorado Department of Transportation grader was noted leaving the CDOT facility in nearby Gateway and headed toward the area of this flash flooding. No reports of additional damage, or any injuries or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system had moved through eastern Utah and western Colorado in the days before this event, pumping anomalously rich moisture into southern portions of the region. While showers had lingered through the night into the morning of the 3rd, the clouds did not inhibit the development of instability through the morning hours. This instability ultimately led to the development of some strong to severe thunderstorms across southwest Colorado, producing some debris flows and severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At approximately 6:20 PM MDT, a severe thunderstorm moved southwest of Chimney Rock National Monument, producing heavy rain and hail. This heavy rain fell on an area of very steep terrain and produced a mud flow and small rock slide across Colorado Highway 151. Aside from the road needing to be cleared, no damage, injuries, or fatalities were reported in association with this debris flow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet spring storm had moved through western Colorado in the days before this event, bringing mountain snow, as well as mixed rain and snow in the lower elevation valleys. Particularly, in the southern Colorado valleys, rain and snow allowed for increased surface moisture to become trapped. As skies cleared and temperatures cooled, this trapped moisture condensed, becoming patchy dense fog across the southwestern Colorado valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: According to the KDRO ASOS in Durango, dense fog developed at the airport around 1:15 AM local time, with visibility rapidly dropping to 1/4 mile or less. The KDRO ASOS continued to report this very low visibility through approximately 7:30 AM local time, at which point visibility began to gradually improve. The fog had fully lifted by 9:00AM local time. Satellite imagery also indicated this area of dense fog stretching along the lower elevations of the zone, close to the New Mexico border, and extending into neighboring forecast zones. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received in association with this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet spring storm had moved through western Colorado in the days before this event, bringing mountain snow, as well as mixed rain and snow in the lower elevation valleys. Particularly, in the southern Colorado valleys, rain and snow allowed for increased surface moisture to become trapped. As skies cleared and temperatures cooled, this trapped moisture condensed, becoming patchy dense fog across the southwestern Colorado valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog that produced visibility of 1/4 mile or less at the KDRO ASOS was observed, via satellite, to extend into the adjacent Colorado Forecast Zone 023. The nearest surface observation points in this zone, including the KPSO AWOS, were well north of the patch of dense fog, which had remained in the river basin close to the New Mexico border. However, with the visibilities produced in the adjacent zone, it is highly likely that these conditions extended, at least locally, into this zone as well. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received related to this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong spring storm moved through the area on April 1st, bringing mountain snow and valley rain to the region, as well as much cooler temperatures in the wake of the associated cold front. Ahead of the cold front, the tightened pressure gradient aloft led to widespread strong, gusty southwesterly winds across the region. Some gusts across the higher elevations reached the 60-70 mph range, while valley locations gusted 50-60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Carbonate Hill mesonet site, located above 12000 feet in elevation, recorded a gust to 75 mph at approximately 2AM local time on the morning of April 1st. Wind gusts across the zone during the morning hours of April 1st ranged from 45 mph up to the peak gust of 75 mph. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received in association with this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two supercell thunderstorms tracked eastward across the western part of Elbert County during the afternoon of May 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report from Mping.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous supercell severe thunderstorms tracked across eastern Colorado. Most of the reports were large hail, up to 3 inches in diameter. Most reports were from Lincoln County where numerous storm chasers were.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The report was from mPING.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second day in a row, severe thunderstorms develop over the Palmer Divide and tracked eastward across Lincoln. Hail up to golf ball size (1.75 inches) fell and winds reached up to 76 mph. A brief tornado was also reported near Elizabeth. Additional thunderstorms formed to the north along a convergence zone and produced hail up to 1 inch in diameter over Weld County.

A trough dropping out of the Pacific Northwest on the 20th helped to tighten the pressure gradient aloft. This in turn worked to increase surface winds across eastern Utah and western Colorado. In addition, a ridge of high pressure that was still partly in place on the 20th promoted clear skies and deep mixing, helping to mix down even stronger winds to the surface. This pattern lingered into the afternoon of the 21st, allowing for a few late wind gusts to pop up at higher elevations of northwest Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Colorado Department of Transportation road weather mesonet site at Douglas Pass recorded multiple gusts above 75 mph throughout the afternoon of the 20th. The first was a gust to 78 mph at 11:39 AM MDT, followed by the peak gust to 89 mph at 1:31 PM MDT, and an additional gust to 75 mph at 5:56 PM MDT. Other gusts around the zone were in the upper 40s and 50s mph. The Douglas Pass site is one of the highest sensors in the zone, and is oriented along a north-south axis that does well in wind regimes such as this one, explaining the particularly high wind gusts achieved at this location. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received associated with this event.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Colorado Department of Transportation road weather mesonet site 070w001 Utah State Line recorded a gust to 60 mph at approximately 1:21 PM MDT on the 20th. Other gusts around the region ranged from the upper 40s mph to the mid 50s mph. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

Ahead of the cold front, the tightened pressure gradient aloft led to widespread strong, gusty southwesterly winds across the region. Some gusts across the higher elevations reached the 60-70 mph range, while valley locations gusted 50-60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At approximately 2:15 AM local time, the KRIL ASOS at Garfield County Regional Airport recorded a gust to 59 mph. Gusts across the zone during the morning of April 1st ranged from 45 mph to the peak gust of 59 mph. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received in association with this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another spring storm had moved into the Pacific Northwest and was set to track across the Great Basin on the 12th. Ahead of this system, the pressure gradient aloft had begun to tighten, strengthening southwesterly winds at the surface. In addition to this, a strong upper level jet was set to move overhead, and the warm weather during the day would allow for deep mixing to bring down even stronger gusts from this jet. All combined to produce widespread wind gusts of 34-45 mph, with higher gusts across the higher elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At approximately 1:15PM local time on April 12th, the Basalt-Holland Hills mesonet station recorded a gust to 75 mph. Other gusts across the zone ranged from 45 mph to the peak of 75 mph. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received in association with this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As is often the case with high wind events across eastern Utah and western Colorado, this event was driven by an approaching low pressure system that was tightening the pressure gradient aloft ahead of it. A potent jet streak was moving through the southwesterly flow ahead of this system as well, and with a dry streak moving into the region, deep mixing during the day on the 17th was anticipated to tap into the stronger winds of this jet and mix them down. A third contributing factor to the strong winds on the 17h was the approaching cold front, which had stalled out over northeast Utah and northwest Colorado, driving strong southwesterly surface winds out ahead of it. All together, this event led to widespread gusts of 45-55 mph across eastern Utah and the lower elevations of western Colorado, with several gusts of 60 mph or better.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The KGJT ASOS at the Grand Junction Regional Airport recorded a gust to 59 mph at approximately 6:30 AM local time on April 17th. Gusts across the zone ranged from 45 mph to the peak of 59 mph through much of the day on the 17th, before diminishing in the evening as the cold front finally moved through. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received in association with this event.

A trough dropping out of the Pacific Northwest on the 20th helped to tighten the pressure gradient aloft. This in turn worked to increase surface winds across eastern Utah and western Colorado. In addition, a ridge of high pressure that was still partly in place on the 20th promoted clear skies and deep mixing, helping to mix down even stronger winds to the surface. This pattern lingered into the afternoon of the 21st, allowing for a few late wind gusts to pop up at higher elevations of northwest Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At approximately 2:08 PM MDT, the KCAG ASOS at Craig-Moffat County Airport recorded a gust to 62 mph. This was the peak gust of the day, but additional gusts between 58 mph and the peak of 62 mph were recorded at both the Pinto, Massadona 18SE RAWS and the Great Divide 2SW RAWS. Additional sensors around the zone recorded gusts above 45 mph through the afternoon. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received in association with this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Four tornadoes occurred just to the south and east of metro Denver in the early afternoon of Sunday, May 18th.||The first tornado that produced damage in northwest Elbert County was an uncharacteristically strong landspout, as well as the last tornado that occurred northeast of Bennett. The other two tornadoes were more strongly associated with a parent thunderstorm, known as a supercell.||The first is for the initial tornado, rated EF2, in and near the Elkhorn neighborhood of extreme northwest Elbert County, Colorado. The second is an EF1 that occurred south|of Manila Village. The third and fourth tornadoes were both ranked EF2. These tornadoes tracked just to the west and north of Bennett, Colorado. They were two separate tornadoes, and were both on the ground at the same time between 140 pm and 146 pm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report from Mping.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms with strong damaging winds tracked across northeast Colorado during the afternoon and evening of May 14th. High winds of 60-80 mph reported in numerous locations, and at least a couple reports of trees that fell onto homes. One in Brush, CO, and two more homes had damage due to fallen trees in Holyoke, CO. Power outages were reported in Holyoke, Iliff, Brush, and some surrounding rural locations in northeast Colorado. Strong winds from a cold front (enhanced from the thunderstorm outflow winds) pushed through eastern Elbert and Lincoln Counties with gusts of 55 to 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County dispatch reported two trees down on houses within Holyoke, along with other tree damage and power outages. Time estimated.

A strong supercell thunderstorm tracked south-southeast across far northeast Weld, southwest Logan and northwest Washington Counties. The storms produced large hail, up to 3 inches in diameter and also three tornadoes across Logan and Washington Counties during the afternoon hours of May 23rd, including one EF-2 and one EF-1. Most of the observed damage was snapped power poles and trees. One single family residence sustained EF-1 damage, but there were no injuries reported. This supercell also produced a swath of golf ball to baseball sized hail, with one ICECHIP report of over 3 inch diameter hail in northern Washington county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report from Mping.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: By the middle of April, most lower elevation forecast zones had hit the 50 growing degree day thresholds, meaning that the growing season was well underway. Much of this was due to a period of above normal temperatures the week prior. However, a strong spring storm with a potent cold front moved through the area from the 17th to the 18th, bringing in much colder air. As skies cleared and winds dropped on the night of the 18th into the morning of the 19th, temperatures began to plummet across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The coldest temperature recorded at the KRIL ASOS at Garfield County Regional Airport was 30F, while the coldest temperature recorded at any of the 8 available sensors was 27F. A total of 5 out of the 8 available sensors recorded at least 3 hours of temperatures at or below 32F, with an average of 4 hours, a maximum of 10 hours, and a minimum of 0 hours. These sensors averaged less than 1 hour at or below 28F, disqualifying it as a hard freeze event. The maximum period at or below 28F was 3 hours exactly, meaning that a few locations likely experienced localized hard freeze conditions. No reports of crop damages or losses were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed low pressure system over the Pacific Northwest brought a strong cold front across the area that moved through from northwest to southeast. Light to moderate snowfall fell over most mountain areas of eastern Utah and western Colorado with heaviest amounts favored among the Uncompahgre Gorge of the northwest San Juans as well as some northwest favored slopes. The northwest Colorado valleys also saw light to moderate snowfall with lesser amounts elsewhere. In addition strong winds led to impacts due to blowing snow and low visibility with gusts in the 60 to 80 mph range across some higher mountain areas and gusts of 40 to 60 mph across the northwest Colorado valleys in addition to the snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 5 to 12 inches of snow fell across the area with up to 13 inches of snow at Rabbit Ears Pass. Wind gusts at Storm Peak Lab reached 59 mph before the event started but gusted to 49 mph during the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed low pressure system over the Pacific Northwest brought a strong cold front across the area that moved through from northwest to southeast. Light to moderate snowfall fell over most mountain areas of eastern Utah and western Colorado with heaviest amounts favored among the Uncompahgre Gorge of the northwest San Juans as well as some northwest favored slopes. The northwest Colorado valleys also saw light to moderate snowfall with lesser amounts elsewhere. In addition strong winds led to impacts due to blowing snow and low visibility with gusts in the 60 to 80 mph range across some higher mountain areas and gusts of 40 to 60 mph across the northwest Colorado valleys in addition to the snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 5 to 12 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts up to 16 inches at McCoy Park. 7 inches of snow was reported at Vail 0.9 WNW. Winds generally gusted 45 to 70 mph at elevations above 11,000 feet with a peak wind gust of 89 mph at Snowmass Ski Area Baldy on April 1 at 00:00. During the March 31 portion of the event, wind gusts at Snowmass Ski Area Baldy were in the 70 to 75 mph range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Once the growing season is underway, freeze products are needed even when a widespread freeze or hard freeze has occurred as recently as the night before, if conditions warrant. Such was the case on the night of the 19th to the 20th, as continued cold air advection behind a departing low pressure combined with good radiational cooling conditions to lead to widespread temperatures at or below 32F in the lower valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The KRIL ASOS at the Garfield County Regional Airport recorded a minimum temperature of 32F, while the minimum at any of the 8 available sensors was 21F. A total of 4 of the available 8 sensors recorded at least 3 hours of temperatures at or below 32F, with an average 3.5 hours, a maximum of 9 hours, and a minimum of 0 hours. Therefore this qualified as a standard freeze event. Temperatures were at or below 28F for an average of only 1 hour, which does not meet the 3 hour minimum to qualify as a hard freeze. However, the maximum duration of temperatures being below 28F was 7 hours, meaning that localized hard freeze conditions did occur. No reports of crop damages or losses were received.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The minimum temperature recorded at the KMTJ ASOS at the Montrose Regional Airport was 28F, while the minimum recorded at any of the available 14 sensors was 23F. A total of 13 out of the 14 sensors recorded at least 3 hours of temperatures at or below 32F, with an average duration of 6 hours, a maximum of 9 hours and a minimum of 2 hours. CZ_NAME: PARADOX VALLEY / LOWER DOLORES RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Once the growing season is underway, freeze products are needed even when a widespread freeze or hard freeze has occurred as recently as the night before, if conditions warrant. Such was the case on the night of the 19th to the 20th, as continued cold air advection behind a departing low pressure combined with good radiational cooling conditions to lead to widespread temperatures at or below 32F in the lower valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There are no ASOS's or AWOS's in this zone. The minimum temperature recorded at a RAWS station was 15F, which was also the minimum temperature recorded at any of the available 7 sensors. This occurred at the Dry Creek RAWS station. All 7 of the available sensors recorded at least 3 hours at or below 32F, with an average duration of 9.5 hours, a maximum of 11 hours and a minimum of 6 hours. This qualifies this event as a standard freeze. Temperatures at or below 28F were recorded for an average of 6 hours, with a maximum duration of 9 hours and a minimum of 1 hour. This qualifies this event as a hard freeze event as well. No reports of crop damages or losses were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Once the growing season is underway, freeze products are needed even when a widespread freeze or hard freeze has occurred as recently as the night before, if conditions warrant. Such was the case on the night of the 19th to the 20th, as continued cold air advection behind a departing low pressure combined with good radiational cooling conditions to lead to widespread temperatures at or below 32F in the lower valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The minimum temperature recorded at the KDRO ASOS at Durango-La Plata County Airport was 27F, while the minimum temperature recorded at any of the 17 available sensors was 23F. All 17 available sensors recorded at least 3 hours of temperatures at or below 32F, with an average duration of 9.5 hours. The maximum duration was 11 hours and the minimum was 7. Therefore this event qualified as a standard freeze. Temperatures at or below 28F were recorded for, on average, 4 hours, with a maximum duration of 8 hours and a minimum of 1 hour. Therefore this event also qualifies as a hard freeze event. No reports of crop damages or losses were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While other areas of western Colorado and eastern Utah had begun to see temperatures rebound, both during the day and over night, the southwestern Colorado valleys remained cold. In particular, La Plata county and some of the surrounding areas saw one last night of widespread freeze conditions thanks to the good radiational cooling conditions experienced.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The KDRO ASOS at the Durango-La Plata County Airport was 28F, while the minimum recorded at any of the available 17 sensors was 15F. A total of 15 out of the available 17 sensors recorded temperatures at or below 32F for at least 3 hours, with an average duration of 5 hours. The maximum duration was 8 hours while the minimum was 0. This event, therefore, qualifies as a freeze event. Temperatures at or below 28F were recorded for an, on average, 2 hours, which is below the 3 hour minimum required to qualify as a hard freeze event. However, the maximum duration of temperatures at or below 28F was 6 hours, meaning that localized hard freeze conditions were experienced in the zone. No reports of crop damages or losses were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system had moved through eastern Utah and western Colorado in the days before this event, pumping anomalously rich moisture into southern portions of the region. While showers had lingered through the night into the morning of the 3rd, the clouds did not inhibit the development of instability through the morning hours. This instability ultimately led to the development of some strong to severe thunderstorms across southwest Colorado, producing some debris flows and severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tracked southeast from 11 miles southwest of Dove Creek from eastern San Juan County, Utah through west-central Dolores County and into northwestern Montezuma County in Colorado. This storm produced hail up to 1.5 inches in diameter, which fell over southwest Dolores county along the county roads southwest of Dove Creek. The photo was taken of the golf ball size hail after the hail fell. Radar was used for the approximate start and end times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With abundant moisture present, eastern Utah and western Colorado were primed to see widespread thunderstorm activity. A shortwave moved through the area in the afternoon, with a strong cold front associated with it. The cold air advecting in aloft helped to steepen lapse rates, and enough sunshine had gotten through during the morning to produce CAPE values of 500-1000 J/kg - more than enough to produce strong storms west of the Divide. As a result, a number of strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and produced severe hail and flash flooding across central western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Colorado Department of Transportation closed down both lanes of Colorado Highway 145 at mile marker 95 due to a mudslide around 1:35 PM MDT. This closure was lifted later that afternoon, once the road was cleared of mud. No reports of damages, fatalities, or injuries were received other than the temporary road closure.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 1625 and 1650 MST, a severe storm tracked southeast through the Grand Valley from Fruita through Orchard Mesa, including the Redlands and Grand Junction, with numerous reports of severe hail from 1 inch to 1.75 inches in diameter, which is up to golf ball size. Multiple injuries occurred as a result of the large hail at the Mesa County Fairgrounds where an outdoor concert event was being held. Multiple reports of damage to windshields, roofs and siding of homes as well. The extent of injuries and damage is unknown at this time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A much drier airmass was working in across eastern Utah and western Colorado on the afternoon of June 12th. This allowed for drier air to infiltrate surface levels, while enough moisture remained aloft to produce showers and thunderstorms. These two factors combined to create strong and efficient outflow winds that were capable of producing severe gusts in excess of 58 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Dinosaur NM 19N RAWS recorded a gust to 62 mph at approximately 8:22 PM MDT on June 12th. This gust was associated with a small thunderstorm that had formed over the Dinosaur National Monument area. This was the only gust recorded in the area, and no reports of damages, injuries, or fatalities were received associated with it.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Great Divide 2N RAWS recorded a wind gust to 60 mph at approximately 5:46 PM MDT on June 12th. This gust was associated with a passing band of showers and thunderstorms. No reports of damages, injuries, or fatalities were recorded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms with strong damaging winds tracked across northeast Colorado during the afternoon and evening of May 14th. High winds of 60-80 mph reported in numerous locations, and at least a couple reports of trees that fell onto homes. One in Brush, CO, and two more homes had damage due to fallen trees in Holyoke, CO. Power outages were reported in Holyoke, Iliff, Brush, and some surrounding rural locations in northeast Colorado. Strong winds from a cold front (enhanced from the thunderstorm outflow winds) pushed through eastern Elbert and Lincoln Counties with gusts of 55 to 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station US 385 south of Holyoke.

One in Brush, CO, and two more homes had damage due to fallen trees in Holyoke, CO. Power outages were reported in Holyoke, Iliff, Brush, and some surrounding rural locations in northeast Colorado. Strong winds from a cold front (enhanced from the thunderstorm outflow winds) pushed through eastern Elbert and Lincoln Counties with gusts of 55 to 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured at the Akron Airport, AKO.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest flow over the area allowed for a isolated supercell to develop along the Rampart Mountains and then push to the southeast across Colorado Springs, bringing damaging hail to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to 1 inches in diameter fell in El Paso County from severe thunderstorms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Four tornadoes occurred just to the south and east of metro Denver in the early afternoon of Sunday, May 18th.||The first tornado that produced damage in northwest Elbert County was an uncharacteristically strong landspout, as well as the last tornado that occurred northeast of Bennett. The other two tornadoes were more strongly associated with a parent thunderstorm, known as a supercell.||The first is for the initial tornado, rated EF2, in and near the Elkhorn neighborhood of extreme northwest Elbert County, Colorado. The second is an EF1 that occurred south|of Manila Village. The third and fourth tornadoes were both ranked EF2. These tornadoes tracked just to the west and north of Bennett, Colorado. They were two separate tornadoes, and were both on the ground at the same time between 140 pm and 146 pm.

A strong supercell thunderstorm tracked south-southeast across far northeast Weld, southwest Logan and northwest Washington Counties. The storms produced large hail, up to 3 inches in diameter and also three tornadoes across Logan and Washington Counties during the afternoon hours of May 23rd, including one EF-2 and one EF-1. Most of the observed damage was snapped power poles and trees. One single family residence sustained EF-1 damage, but there were no injuries reported. This supercell also produced a swath of golf ball to baseball sized hail, with one ICECHIP report of over 3 inch diameter hail in northern Washington county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report from Mping.

A snow band developed across portions of eastern Colorado during the morning hours of February 21st leading to 4 to 8 inches of snow. The highest amount was 8 inches in Burlington. Seven miles NNW of Peconic and Bethune reported 5 inches, along with 4 inches in Stratton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report of an estimated 9 inches of snow 10 miles northwest of Burlington. In the town of Burlington 8 inches of snowfall was reported on both a previously dry sidewalk and driveway. Additional amounts of 4-6 inches were reported across the county, with 6 inches reported 12 N of Burlington, 5 inches occurring 8 NNE Burlington, and in Bethune and in Stratton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Conditions ran warmer than normal in the month of June, but enough moisture made it into eastern Utah and western Colorado to at least prevent a significant worsening of the drought. However, there was very little improvement in terms of the extent of Extreme (D3) Drought, with all the zones that began the month with Extreme (D3) Drought conditions also finishing the month with them.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) Drought conditions have only been analyzed along the far northwestern portion of the zone. However, that area remained largely unchanged during the month of June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Rim Road Fire started in late July 8 miles southwest of Ignacio, Colorado. The cause is still under investigation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rim Road Fire started in late July 8 miles southwest of Ignacio, Colorado. The cause is still under investigation. The fire grew to 97 acres before it was contained in mid August. No homes were lost but one outbuilding was destroyed in the fire. Some oil and gas operations were temporarily shut down as a precaution due to the fire's proximity. The estimated cost to date was $740,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The South Rim Fire started in early July due to multiple lightning strikes 9 miles east of Montrose, Colorado which ignited fires on both the north and south rim of the Black Canyon of the Gunnison National Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The South Rim Fire started in early July due to multiple lightning strikes 9 miles east of Montrose, Colorado which ignited fires on both the north and south rim of the Black Canyon of the Gunnison National Park. The South Rim Fire became the larger of the two, which grew to 4,232 acres before it was contained in early to mid September. The North Rim Fire was quickly put out one day after it started on July 11th. The north rim of the park was reopened to the public July 30th, while the south rim reopened to the public on August 18th. The estimated cost to date was $15,300,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Due to ongoing construction in and around the Grand Junction Regional Airport, days with particularly strong winds and/or outflows from nearby thunderstorms can trigger localized dust storms that reduce the visibility to less than 1 mile. Typically these events last less than 20 minutes. However, on this day the reduction in visibility was to below 1/4 mile and lasted for approximately 2 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The KGJT ASOS located the Grand Junction Regional Airport recorded a 2 hour period where visibility was less than 2 miles, with periods of visibility as low as 1/4 mile. During this period, sustained winds of 25-40 mph were recorded, with gusts up to 51 mph. Ongoing construction in and around the airport did contribute to the available dust to blow around, but the extended nature of this event warranted inclusion. No reports of impacts were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong microburst hit Denver International Airport in a rare early morning event. Damage occurred to a Delta Airlines plane parked at the airport. The strong winds knocked the plane out of the chocks, and rotated it 90 degrees. Damage occurred to the nose cone as it hit another object, and the aircraft will be out of service. Four other wind shear sensors on the airport grounds reported gusts in the 50-58 kt range. Additional high-based thunderstorms produce winds to around 60 mph during the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong microburst hit Denver International Airport in a rare early morning event. Damage occurred to a Delta Airlines plane parked at the airport. The strong winds knocked the plane out of the chocks, and rotated it 90 degrees. Damage occurred to the nose cone as it hit another object, and the aircraft will be out of service. Information was relayed to us by Dave Cunningham, Director of Operations at DIA. He initially had inquired about the winds at the KDEN ASOS, with a brief but high wind gust of 70 mph (61 kts) reported at 410 AM MDT. Four other wind shear sensors on the airport grounds reported gusts in the 50-58 kt range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A somewhat classic monsoonal pattern set up in the days leading up to this flash flooding event. A high centered over the Southern Plains and a low off the West Coast, drawing tropical and subtropical moisture northward out of Mexico and the tropical Pacific ocean. In addition, a weak jet streak moving through the flow overhead helped provide some extra energy to afternoon showers and thunderstorms. By the afternoon, a line of showers and thunderstorms developed stretching from near Monticello, UT to Grand Junction, CO. Along this line lay the La Sal mountains, which provided some upslope enhancement to these storms. On the other side of the La Sals is the Dolores River valley, and the town of Gateway, where flash flooding occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Training storms off the La Sals resulted in waterfalls off terrain just southeast of Gateway (approx 1900 UTC). No impacts other than inundating basecamp for the Turner Gulch fire, which was set up in a floodplain along the Dolores river, southeast of town (by bridge with USGS gage). Flooding was not associated with the ongoing Turner Gulch Fire to the west of the flood location. Reports associated with this event were provided by fire personnel at the Turner Gulch Basecamp. While the land around the basecamp was inundated, the camp itself was not impacted, and conditions dried out enough that no firefighting apparatus was stuck in the mud. Therefore there were no impacts or damages associated with this event. There were also no injuries or fatalities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Stoner Mesa Fire started in late July due to a lightning strike 11 miles northeast of Dolores, Colorado to the northeast of Stoner, Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Stoner Mesa Fire started in late July due to a lightning strike 11 miles northeast of Dolores, Colorado to the northeast of Stoner, Colorado. The fire was initially out but re-emerged in early August and grew to 10,249 acres before it was contained in mid October. The estimated cost was $25,015,851.  693 structures were threatened but none were destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of 2-3 inches led to flooded roadways and parts of I-25 shutdown around and a few miles northeast of Colorado City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The initial front of the storm brought sustained winds of approximately 75 mph, confirmed via Kestrel measurement. These winds were accompanied by a torrential downpour in Colorado City, resulting in rapid flooding in the immediate area. The initial downpour lasted approximately 45 minutes and produced street and highway flooding, with standing and moving water measured at approximately 3-5 inches in most areas, generally still traversable by passenger vehicles. Approximately 15 minutes after the rain began to lessen, reports of flooding were received for Interstate 25 at Milepost 77 and Milepost 70. Milepost 70: Flooding slowed traffic but did not result in any reported traffic accidents.  Milepost 77: Flooding rendered the interstate temporarily impassable to most vehicles, with large debris present on the roadway and several traffic accidents reported. Within approximately two hours after the storm passed, water had fully receded from all roadways, and no further significant closures or incidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong supercell thunderstorm tracked south-southeast across far northeast Weld, southwest Logan and northwest Washington Counties. The storms produced large hail, up to 3 inches in diameter and also three tornadoes across Logan and Washington Counties during the afternoon hours of May 23rd, including one EF-2 and one EF-1. Most of the observed damage was snapped power poles and trees. One single family residence sustained EF-1 damage, but there were no injuries reported. This supercell also produced a swath of golf ball to baseball sized hail, with one ICECHIP report of over 3 inch diameter hail in northern Washington county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The In-situ Collaborative Experiment for the Collection of Hail In the Plains (ICECHIP) reported the hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As the month of July continued to be hot and dry, drought worsened across the region, and Extreme (D3) Drought conditions expanded into new areas at the end of the month. This mainly included portions of northwestern Colorado and northeastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Drought Monitor analysis at the end of July included a patch of Extreme (D3) Drought conditions in Colorado Zone 001, Lower Yampa River Basin. This was in northwest Colorado, near the areas of northeast Utah where Extreme (D3) Drought had also developed by the time of this update. The continued hot and dry conditions, especially over the lower elevations, likely contributed to this expansion. No reports of crop damages or failures, or other drought related impacts, were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Deer Creek Fire started in early July due to human causes 1 mile north of Old La Sal, Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Deer Creek Fire started in early July due to human causes 1 mile north of Old La Sal, Utah. The fire grew to 17,724 acres and spread into portions of west-central Colorado particularly western Montrose county, before it was contained in early August. The estimated cost to date was $24,021,755. 11 private structures, a U.S. Forest Service guard station and communication site were destroyed in the fire shortly after it started. A fire induced tornado rated EF2 also occurred on the Deer Creek Fire on July 12, with wind speeds up to 122 mph.

The 8/20/25 Drought Monitor update expanded the Extreme (D3) Drought conditions to cover the remainder of the zone, meaning the entire zone now had Extreme (D3) Drought or worse conditions. Severe thunderstorms with strong damaging winds tracked across northeast Colorado during the afternoon and evening of May 14th. High winds of 60-80 mph reported in numerous locations, and at least a couple reports of trees that fell onto homes. One in Brush, CO, and two more homes had damage due to fallen trees in Holyoke, CO. Power outages were reported in Holyoke, Iliff, Brush, and some surrounding rural locations in northeast Colorado. Strong winds from a cold front (enhanced from the thunderstorm outflow winds) pushed through eastern Elbert and Lincoln Counties with gusts of 55 to 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Raws station Crow Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong microburst hit Denver International Airport in a rare early morning event. Damage occurred to a Delta Airlines plane parked at the airport. The strong winds knocked the plane out of the chocks, and rotated it 90 degrees. Damage occurred to the nose cone as it hit another object, and the aircraft will be out of service. Four other wind shear sensors on the airport grounds reported gusts in the 50-58 kt range. Additional high-based thunderstorms produce winds to around 60 mph during the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CDOT weather sensor at I-70 and York Street.

CZ_NAME: PARADOX VALLEY / LOWER DOLORES RIVER BASIN
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The month saw above normal temperatures and generally below normal precipitation across eastern Utah and western Colorado. What little moisture did manage to move into the region favored the higher terrain, allowing drought conditions across the lower elevations to worsen through the month. A brief period of moisture at the beginning of the month contributed to brief drought improvements for certain areas, but the overall hot and dry month led to expansion of drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the beginning of the month of July, the US Drought Monitor analyzed an area of Extreme (D3) Drought in Colorado Zone 020 - Paradox Valley/Lower Dolores River Basin. Some improved moisture early in the month allowed for a significant reduction in the areal extent of Extreme (D3) Drought with the July 9th Drought Monitor update. After this point, conditions remained largely unchanged. No reports of crop damage or failures, or other drought related impacts, aside from large wildfires that developed, were received.

However, Severe (D2) Drought remained, and Extreme (D3) Drought did redevelop in early August. No reports of crop damage or failures, or other drought related impacts, were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong microburst hit Denver International Airport in a rare early morning event. Damage occurred to a Delta Airlines plane parked at the airport. The strong winds knocked the plane out of the chocks, and rotated it 90 degrees. Damage occurred to the nose cone as it hit another object, and the aircraft will be out of service. Four other wind shear sensors on the airport grounds reported gusts in the 50-58 kt range. Additional high-based thunderstorms produce winds to around 60 mph during the afternoon hours.

The month of September continued to run above normal in terms of temperatures, and below normal in terms of precipitation. However, there were a few periods of heavy rain across the region, particularly around September 12th, and again at the end of the month, which helped to improve drought conditions across the region. A dry end to the month did allow for some worsening drought around the Four Corners.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the beginning of the month of September, the US Drought Monitor analyzed an area of Exceptional (D4) Drought conditions as covering roughly 75% of Colorado Zone 008- Central Colorado River Basin, with the remaining 25% of the zone under Extreme (D3) Drought conditions. The area of Exceptional (D4) Drought was removed with the 9/3 Drought Monitor update, with 100% of the zone now under Extreme (D3) Drought conditions. There was no substantial change to drought conditions from this point through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture was slowly increasing around an area of high pressure to our west, allowing for diurnal showers and storms to form over the high terrain and drift into some lower valleys. Some of these decaying showers produced strong outflow wind gusts even with no lightning present over Rangely Airport with a gust to 58 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 58 mph occurred at the Rangely Airport at 1535 MST due to a decaying shower with no lightning present.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough over the Great Basin pulled anomalous deep moisture into western Colorado with a threat for excessive rainfall with any storm activity. One particular storm tracked over the Redstone area, resulting in a mudslide on Highway 133 due to heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mudslide occurred on Highway 133 due to heavy rainfall from a thunderstorm at mile marker 59.5. Radar estimated that 0.7 to 1.4 inches of rain fell within the drainage above Highway 133. The mudslide was reported at 1530 MST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the beginning of the month of September, the US Drought Monitor analyzed an area of Exceptional (D4) Drought conditions covering roughly 1/3 of Colorado Zone 002 - Central Yampa River Basin, with the remainder of the zone under Extreme (D3) Drought conditions. The area of Exceptional (D4) Drought was removed with the 9/3 Drought Monitor update, with the area covered by Extreme (D3) conditions reduced to roughly 80% of the zone. A slight reduction in the area covered by Extreme (D3) conditions was noted with the 9/10 update, but a more substantial reduction came with the 9/17 update, where the coverage of Extreme (D3) Drought was reduced to just 20% of the zone. The 9/24 update saw the area of Extreme (D3) Drought reduced further to just 15% coverage. No changes to drought conditions were noted between this point and the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The month of September continued to run above normal in terms of temperatures, and below normal in terms of precipitation. However, there were a few periods of heavy rain across the region, particularly around September 12th, and again at the end of the month, which helped to improve drought conditions across the region. A dry end to the month did allow for some worsening drought around the Four Corners.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the beginning of the month of September, the US Drought Monitor analyzed an area of Extreme (D3) Drought conditions that covered 100% of Colorado Zone 005 - Upper Yampa River Basin. With the 9/3 Drought Monitor update, Extreme (D3) Drought coverage was reduced to approximately 40% of the zone. Drought conditions remained largely unchanged from this point to the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms produced widespread strong winds across the northeast Corner of Colorado across the counties of Logan, Phillips, and Sedgwick.

The period between the 11th and 13th of September had a relatively unique meteorological setup for western Colorado and eastern Utah. A potent closed low was located over Southern California, and a ridge of high pressure was located to our southeast, directing a rich plume of monsoonal moisture northward through the Four Corners. Mixing ratios in this plume of moisture were more than high enough to not only help form strong to severe thunderstorms, but also to produce flash flooding and debris flows. Additionally, there was ample instability, with morning clearing helping 600-1000 J/kg of CAPE develop each afternoon. The region also fell under the right exit region of a potent upper level jet streak, which added additional lift and 40-50 kts of shear. Additionally, terrain effects added additional lift and shear. All together, this was a potent setup with the potential to produce severe thunderstorms. And indeed, the storms that developed produced not only large hail and strong, damaging winds, but also two tornadoes in Southeast Utah, and flash flooding across the area. Particularly, fresh burn scars, including the Lee and Elk RBX scars, saw substantial flash flooding and debris flows as storms trained over the area and produced very heavy rain. Finally, after 3 days of widespread severe weather, the cold front pushed through the region, and a cooler, drier, and above all, calmer airmass moved in, bringing an end to the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 1452 MST on September 11, a trained spotter reported 1 inch diameter hail 2 miles NNW of Falfa, Colorado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Derby Fire started in mid August due to a lightning strike 13.5 miles north of Dotsero, Colorado. The fire grew to 5,453 acres spreading across Eagle and Garfield counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Derby Fire started in mid August due to a lightning strike 13.5 miles north of Dotsero, Colorado. The fire grew to 5,453 acres spreading across Eagle and Garfield counties. While major suppression efforts ended in October and significant fire activity ceased, the remaining 5 percent was in an inaccessible area with pockets of interior smoke. It was officially 100 percent contained by mid December. The estimated cost was $23,860,000.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the beginning of the month of September, the US Drought Monitor analyzed an around 10% area of Exceptional (D4) Drought in Colorado Zone 001 - Lower Yampa River Basin, with the remainder of the zone under Extreme (D3) Drought conditions. With the 9/3 Drought Monitor update, the area of Exception (D4) Drought was removed, with the entire zone now under Extreme (D3) conditions. With the 9/17 update, there was significant improvement, with the area of Extreme (D3) Drought conditions reduced to covering only 1/3 of the zone. The 9/24 update saw yet more improvement, with Extreme (D3) conditions reduced to covering just 25% of the zone. There was no change from that point to the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On September 4th, precipitable water values increased to above normal values, with the 18z Grand Junction sounding showing 0.69 inches of precipitable water. To the west of eastern Utah and western Colorado, an area of weak rotation associated with a passing shortwave helped to provide extra lift. Morning sunshine helped to realized enough instability that, with all other factors present, thunderstorms developed over the higher terrain through the afternoon. These storms were capable of producing heavy rain, were not especially fast moving, and had a tendency to train over the same area for several hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over the Redlands area of Grand Junction late in the afternoon of September 4th, and remained there for 2-3 hours. Impacts began to be noticed around 6:45 PM MDT, with an LSR in the area of 1 ESE Redlands indicating Water out of the banks of nearby wash and running over Redlands Parkway in several areas. Approximately half an hour later, another LSR indicated that water running over the roadway in many areas along South Camp Road in the Redlands (Redlands 1SSE). Debris was also reported as having to be removed from Monument Road (Redlands 3SSE). In addition to the LSR'd events, properties were flooded, normally dry washes were overfilled, and water and debris inundated several roadways in the area. Additionally, social media reports indicated flooding along No Thoroughfare Canyon, Monument Road, and in the Devil's Kitchen area of Colorado National Monument. Once the rain stopped and the storms dissipated, a local spotter provided the total precipitation from this storm, which was a 2.33 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low tracking north of eastern Utah and western Colorado pushed a boundary through the northern counties, helping to trigger numerous showers and thunderstorms. Moisture with this system was rather shallow, but training thunderstorms made up for the lack of moisture, leading to flash flooding potential over sensitive areas. This included the Lee Burn Scar in Rio Blanco County, where numerous debris flows ran on this day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported debris and mud closing both lanes of State Highway 13 at Mile Marker 36. Road had to be cleared before being able to be reopened.
