EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported water and debris clogging a culvert at Mile Marker 1.3 on Rio Blanco County Route 33. The road was considered impassable and required clearing. Clean up crews responded to the area at 9AM the following day (September 23rd, 2025), with the road cleared by the end of the day. This area is adjacent to the Lee Burn Scar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The month of August continued where July left off, with overall above normal temperatures and below normal precipitation. In particular, the lower elevations of eastern Utah and western Colorado showed the greatest departures in both temperatures and precipitation. As a result, Extreme (D3) Drought either remained unchanged in coverage, or expanded through the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the beginning of the month of August, the US Drought Monitor analysis indicated that Extreme (D3) Drought covered the entirety of Colorado Zone 001 - Lower Yampa River Basin. With the 8/13/25 Drought Monitor update, a small sliver (less than 10% of the zone) of Exceptional (D4) Drought developed. After this addition of Exceptional (D4) conditions, the drought conditions in the zone remained unchanged through the end of the month.

CZ_NAME: WEST JACKSON & WEST GRAND COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest flow transported Pacific moisture into the region produce widespread snow across the mountains, heaviest north of I-70. Snotel stations generally reported 8 to 16 inches of snow. The heaviest snowfall from the Snotel stations were over the Park and Gore mountain ranges where an estimate 18 to 28 inches of snow fell. This system was also very windy with ridge top winds reaching 70 to 100 mph. Even at lower elevations winds of 30 to 50 mph were common. This produce considerable blowing and drifting snow. A band of heavier snow accompanied by wind produced brief blizzard conditions during the late morning and early afternoon hours of the 6th as it shift southward. Roads through out the mountains were snow covered. There were brief closures on I-70 due to conditions and traffic accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snotel stations reported an estimated 18 to 28 inches of snowfall. Web camera over Rabbit Ears Pass showed snow covered roads during the day December 6th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Chaser relayed pictures of a tornado.  The tornado was located west of Highway 385 near CR 24. Radar then suggests that the tornado continued across Highway 385 before ending near CR KK and CR 22.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the night a high precipitation storm moved over eastern Colorado, producing rainfall estimates of 3.5 inches, the most of which fell in less than 30 minutes. This caused some basement flash flooding. There was also a report of farm stead having moderate damage from the thunderstorm winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of roof damage, some windows blown out. Stock tanks and dumpsters tossed up to half a mile away to the southeast. A communications tower folded in half. Tons of dead trees and fences blown over and laying to the southeast as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produce one inch hail and a tornado that skipped across the ground over an open field. Dust was lofted into the air at times. The tornado hit a farm. Out of 9 buildings on their property, only one (an old garage) was hit and destroyed. It lifted the roof off the old garage, then the walls collapsed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pencil-thin tornado touched down in southern Weld County then slowly moved south across the county line into Morgan County. Several reports of the tornado were received from the fire department and storm chasers/spotter, and it was hard to discern ground contact through the entire path though one funnel was observed throughout the event. The tornado struck an old garage on a farm property just east of County Road 15 in extreme northern Morgan County. The tornado lifted the roof off the garage, which caused the walls to collapse. No other structures on the property were damaged. The owner was on-site during the tornado and mentioned how narrow the tornado was, and that it lifted not long after hitting the old garage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of August 25th, an unusual amount of moisture was present across eastern Utah and western Colorado. While there was quite a bit of cloud cover present across western portions of the area, skies remained clear over the northern and central Colorado Divide mountains. This helped to develop instability early in the day, which combined with lift from a passing shortwave to trigger some strong thunderstorms during the morning. With the abundant moisture, these storms were heavy rain producers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two rock/mud slides were reported on Highway 133, about 10 miles south of Carbondale, between mile-markers 60 and 63. The road was reported as passable but with mud and debris on the road as of approximately 10:20 AM MDT. Reports from the Colorado Department of Transportation. Road was not closed, but did require clean-up. No reports of injuries or fatalities were received.

CZ_NAME: S & E JACKSON / LARIMER / N & NE GRAND / NW BOULDER COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds developed during the early morning hours of December 9th across the Front Range Mountains and Foothills. These winds strengthened and spread eastward through the morning and early afternoon hours of the 9th. Numerous gusts of 75  to 95 mph were recorded. Power outages between 5,000 to 10,000 were reported.||In the Fairplay area, the intense winds snapped branches and toppled trees. The winds destroyed a chicken coop, which sheared off 4x4 posts supporting a 16-foot section of the structure and scattered debris across his pasture. Highway 285 in Park County was closed for blowing snow and high winds. A CDOT station (Jefferson) along this stretch reported a wind gust of 83 mph at 4:38am on December 9th.||Across northern Larimer County, semi-trucks were blown over on 287 and I-25, causing road closures. The Virginia Dale CDOT weather station reported an 83 mph wind gust and another wind station near this also reported a 87 mph wind gust.  ||A National Weather Service Employee measured a 71 mph wind gust at his backyard sheltered weather station. At least 9 trees uprooted or snapped, with some snapped approximately 20 feet off the ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CDOT station near Lily Lake reported a 71 mph wind gust. There almost no sensors across this zone, but based on nearby reports high winds very likely occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The period between the 11th and 13th of September had a relatively unique meteorological setup for western Colorado and eastern Utah. A potent closed low was located over Southern California, and a ridge of high pressure was located to our southeast, directing a rich plume of monsoonal moisture northward through the Four Corners. Mixing ratios in this plume of moisture were more than high enough to not only help form strong to severe thunderstorms, but also to produce flash flooding and debris flows. Additionally, there was ample instability, with morning clearing helping 600-1000 J/kg of CAPE develop each afternoon. The region also fell under the right exit region of a potent upper level jet streak, which added additional lift and 40-50 kts of shear. Additionally, terrain effects added additional lift and shear. All together, this was a potent setup with the potential to produce severe thunderstorms. And indeed, the storms that developed produced not only large hail and strong, damaging winds, but also two tornadoes in Southeast Utah, and flash flooding across the area. Particularly, fresh burn scars, including the Lee and Elk RBX scars, saw substantial flash flooding and debris flows as storms trained over the area and produced very heavy rain. Finally, after 3 days of widespread severe weather, the cold front pushed through the region, and a cooler, drier, and above all, calmer airmass moved in, bringing an end to the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported flash flooding over Archuleta County Road 500/169 Trujillo Road between Arboles and Caracas. There were additional washouts reported near Arboles point, along Trujillo road about 2 miles southeast from the initial LSR location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unusually rich monsoonal moisture was in place on the morning of August 26th, with precipitable water close to 1.00 inch across eastern Utah and western Colorado. A jet streak moving in aloft brought not only additional moisture, but some extra lift as well. In addition, daytime heating on the complex terrain in this region help to spur on afternoon convection. As a result, widespread shower and thunderstorm activity developed during the afternoon, producing localized very heavy rain and high rainfall rates.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At approximately 1:18 PM MDT, a report reached NWS Grand Junction that heavy rain over the Lee Burn Scar had triggered a debris flow over Rio Blanco County Road 5 in the same area that had run on August 24th. This time, no vehicles were trapped and no other damage occurred, nor were any injuries or fatalities reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low tracking north of eastern Utah and western Colorado pushed a boundary through the northern counties, helping to trigger numerous showers and thunderstorms. Moisture with this system was rather shallow, but training thunderstorms made up for the lack of moisture, leading to flash flooding potential over sensitive areas. This included the Lee Burn Scar in Rio Blanco County, where numerous debris flows ran on this day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported a mudslide at Mile Marker 1.5 on Rio Blanco County Route 5, closing the road and requiring clearing. The debris was cleared within half an hour. This area is within the Lee Burn Scar.

The month of August continued where July left off, with overall above normal temperatures and below normal precipitation. In particular, the lower elevations of eastern Utah and western Colorado showed the greatest departures in both temperatures and precipitation. As a result, Extreme (D3) Drought either remained unchanged in coverage, or expanded through the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the beginning of the month of August, the US Drought Monitor analyzed an area of Extreme (D3) Drought conditions that covered roughly 50% of Colorado Zone 017 - Uncompahgre Plateau and Dallas Divide. Drought conditions remained unchanged until the 8/20/25 US Drought Monitor update, which expanded the area of Extreme (D3) Drought to cover roughly 2/3 of the zone. The coverage of Extreme (D3) Drought conditions then remained unchanged from the 8/20/25 update through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Four tornadoes occurred just to the south and east of metro Denver in the early afternoon of Sunday, May 18th.||The first tornado that produced damage in northwest Elbert County was an uncharacteristically strong landspout, as well as the last tornado that occurred northeast of Bennett. The other two tornadoes were more strongly associated with a parent thunderstorm, known as a supercell.||The first is for the initial tornado, rated EF2, in and near the Elkhorn neighborhood of extreme northwest Elbert County, Colorado. The second is an EF1 that occurred south|of Manila Village. The third and fourth tornadoes were both ranked EF2. These tornadoes tracked just to the west and north of Bennett, Colorado. They were two separate tornadoes, and were both on the ground at the same time between 140 pm and 146 pm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quick moving and rain wrapped tornado swept through Elk Horn Ranch in extreme northern Elbert County before moving into Arapahoe County and lifting. Numerous homes were damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of August 25th, an unusual amount of moisture was present across eastern Utah and western Colorado. While there was quite a bit of cloud cover present across western portions of the area, skies remained clear over the northern and central Colorado Divide mountains. This helped to develop instability early in the day, which combined with lift from a passing shortwave to trigger some strong thunderstorms during the morning. With the abundant moisture, these storms were heavy rain producers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As of approximately 10:10 AM MDT, two rock slides were reported to have occurred on Highway 82 in Snowmass Canyon. No road closures were reported, and no damages occurred. Road did have to be cleared. No injuries or fatalities were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest flow transported Pacific moisture into the region produce widespread snow across the mountains, heaviest north of I-70. Snotel stations generally reported 8 to 16 inches of snow. The heaviest snowfall from the Snotel stations were over the Park and Gore mountain ranges where an estimate 18 to 28 inches of snow fell. This system was also very windy with ridge top winds reaching 70 to 100 mph. Even at lower elevations winds of 30 to 50 mph were common. This produce considerable blowing and drifting snow. A band of heavier snow accompanied by wind produced brief blizzard conditions during the late morning and early afternoon hours of the 6th as it shift southward. Roads through out the mountains were snow covered. There were brief closures on I-70 due to conditions and traffic accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS and COOP observers reported 3 to 8 inches of snow fell across the lower elevations of Grand County. A band of snow and strong winds briefly produced blizzard conditions during the late morning and afternoon hours. Web cameras showed visibility less than a quarter mile in the Winter Park area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds developed during the early morning hours of December 9th across the Front Range Mountains and Foothills. These winds strengthened and spread eastward through the morning and early afternoon hours of the 9th. Numerous gusts of 75  to 95 mph were recorded. Power outages between 5,000 to 10,000 were reported.||In the Fairplay area, the intense winds snapped branches and toppled trees. The winds destroyed a chicken coop, which sheared off 4x4 posts supporting a 16-foot section of the structure and scattered debris across his pasture. Highway 285 in Park County was closed for blowing snow and high winds. A CDOT station (Jefferson) along this stretch reported a wind gust of 83 mph at 4:38am on December 9th.||Across northern Larimer County, semi-trucks were blown over on 287 and I-25, causing road closures. The Virginia Dale CDOT weather station reported an 83 mph wind gust and another wind station near this also reported a 87 mph wind gust.  ||A National Weather Service Employee measured a 71 mph wind gust at his backyard sheltered weather station. At least 9 trees uprooted or snapped, with some snapped approximately 20 feet off the ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An 87 mph wind gust was reported one mile northeast of Crisman. A National Weather Service employee reported a 71 mph wind gust as well as at least 9 trees uprooted or snapped, with some snapped approximately 20 feet off the ground. Across northern Larimer County, semi-trucks were blown over on Highway 287, causing the road to close closures. The Virginia Dale CDOT weather station reported an 83 mph wind gust and another wind station near this also reported a 87 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry end to September and a dry start to October kept drought conditions consistent across much of eastern Utah and western Colorado at the start of the month. However, the arrival of deep tropical moisture from the remnants of Tropical Storms Priscilla and Raymond helped to substantially improve drought conditions, especially across southwest Colorado. In this region, drought was completely eradicated. Only portions of northeast Utah and northwest Colorado, where the deep tropical moisture did not reach, saw drought conditions persist more or less unchanged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the beginning of the month of October, the US Drought Monitor analyzed an area of Extreme (D3) Drought as covering roughly 1% of Colorado Zone 023 - San Juan River Basin, with 50% of the zone under Severe (D2) Drought conditions, and 49% under Moderate (D1) Drought conditions. With the 10/14 update, significant improvements were noted. Extreme (D3) and Severe (D2) Drought conditions were completely removed, and Abnormally Dry (D0) and Drought Free conditions were introduced. Moderate (D1) Drought now covered 5% of the zone, with 90% of the zone under Abnormally Dry (D0) conditions, and 5% under Drought Free conditions. With 10/21 update, Moderate (D1) Drought coverage remained unchanged, but Abnormally Dry (D0) conditions were reduced to 15% coverage. The remaining 80% were now Drought Free. No substantial changes were noted between this point and the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure built over the western CONUS, bringing clear skies and calm winds to the region. Colder northwesterly flow supported lower temperatures in northwestern Colorado, resulting in a freeze for some areas, but not season ending.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As 10 of the 15 reporting stations available for verification in this zone reported temperatures at or below 32F for at least three hours, at least 50% of the zone was below freezing for the required length of time. However, only 3 of the 15 reported temperatures below 28F for at least 3 hours, marking this as a freeze but not a season-ending one. The minimum recorded temperature was 25F at the KCAG ASOS in Craig was 29F, with a range of 25-32F. Temperatures at or below 32F lasted on average for 3 hours, with a maximum of 7 hours and a minimum of 0 Temperatures at or below 28F lasted on average for 1 hour, with a maximum of 3 hours and a minimum of 0.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between October 10-14, 2025, southwest Colorado experienced prolonged periods of rain and some of the largest multiday rainfall totals on record with rainfall accumulations between 3 and 8 inches, with locally higher amounts across the San Juan Mountains. Remnants from two tropical storms supplied anomalous moisture across the region, with minimal break between events. The first round of heavy rainfall resulted in flooding across La Plata and Archuleta counties from decaying Tropical Storm Priscilla beginning October 10 and continuing throughout the day on the 11th. There was a break in rain across the area on October 12, followed by a second round of heavy rain from October 13-14, associated with moisture from remnant Tropical Storm Raymond. Without much time between systems, the hydrologic response of the second storm was much faster than the first. Flooding across several counties in southwest Colorado spiked twice within the 5-day window, leading to major flooding and near record peaks along the Vallecito, Piedra and San Juan watersheds that lingered through the 16th before water levels receded. Numerous debris flows and rockslides also occurred as a result of the abundant rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rock falls occurred along Highway 550 near the Red Mountain Pass snowshed which is between mile markers 87 and 88, due to significant rainfall reported around 1000 MST on October 11.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry air over southwestern Colorado led to clear skies. Overnight, a trough moved through the region, and the associated cold front dropped temperatures to below freezing for much of the area. Under this pattern, three zones experienced a season ending freeze.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sixteen of the twenty-one reporting stations available for verification recording temperatures at or below 32F for at least 3 hours, and eleven of those twenty-one recorded temperatures at or below 28F for at least three hours. This means that at least 50% of the zone experienced a season-ending freeze event. The lowest temperature recorded was 23F, while the lowest recorded at the KEGE AWOS at Eagle was 27F. Minimum temperatures ranged from 23F to 32F. Temperatures at or below 32F lasted for on average 6.5 hours, with a maximum of 11 hours and a minimum of 0. Temperatures at or below 28F lasted for on average 3 hours, with a maximum of 8 hours and a minimum of 0.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A jet streak ahead of a shortwave trough tightened the pressure gradient aloft on October 4th. This, in addition to deep mixing and isolated convection, strengthened southwesterly winds primarily across central and west central Colorado. Most gusts fell below 55 mph, though localized areas of 70-80 mph winds in the mountains and 65-70 mph winds in the valleys were still exhibited.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Colorado Department of Transportation road weather mesonet site at Douglas Pass recorded a wind gust to 75 mph at approximately 10:38 AM MDT on the 4th. A gust to 58 mph was also recorded at the Pine Ridge RAWS site at approximately 12:34 MDT. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received in association with this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry end to September and a dry start to October kept drought conditions consistent across much of eastern Utah and western Colorado at the start of the month. However, the arrival of deep tropical moisture from the remnants of Tropical Storms Priscilla and Raymond helped to substantially improve drought conditions, especially across southwest Colorado. In this region, drought was completely eradicated. Only portions of northeast Utah and northwest Colorado, where the deep tropical moisture did not reach, saw drought conditions persist more or less unchanged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the beginning of the month of October, the US Drought Monitor analyzed an area of Extreme (D3) Drought that covered roughly 30% of Colorado Zone 001 - Lower Yampa River Basin, with the remainder of the zone under Severe (D2) conditions. With the 10/14 Drought Monitor update, significant improvements were noted. Coverage of Extreme (D3) Drought was reduced to 10%, with Severe (D2) Drought covering roughly 40% of the zone, and the introduction of Moderate (D1) conditions that now covered roughly 50% of the zone. With the 10/21 update, there was no change to the coverage of Extreme (D3) conditions, but the coverage of Severe (D2) Drought was reduced to 30%, with 60% of the zone now under Moderate (D1) Drought conditions. There was no change from this point through the end of the month.

With the 10/14 Drought Monitor update, significant improvements were noted. Extreme (D3) Drought was completely removed from the zone, with Severe (D2) Drought now covering 20% of the zone, Moderate (D1) Drought covering 70% of the zone, and the introduction of Abnormally Dry (D0) conditions that covered 10% of the zone. With the 10/21 update, the coverage of Severe (D2) conditions was reduced to 25% of the zone, and the coverage of Abnormally Dry (D0) conditions was increased to 15% of the zone, with the coverage of Moderate (D1) conditions unchanged. There were no substantial changes to Drought conditions in this zone between this point and the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough brought widespread precipitation to western Colorado on the 23rd, leaving behind pools of rich moisture across lower valleys. Subsidence overnight behind the trough cleared skies and cooled overnight lows. Widespread fog was able to develop across many locations across the region as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread fog developed over the Yampa River Basin during the morning of October 6. The KEEO ASOS at the Meeker Coulter Field Airport reported a brief period of patchy dense fog reducing visibility to 1/4 mile before midnight. In the hours around sunrise, fog redeveloped and became more widespread. During this second stint, dense fog reduced visibility to less than 1/4 mile several times. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between October 10-14, 2025, southwest Colorado experienced prolonged periods of rain and some of the largest multiday rainfall totals on record with rainfall accumulations between 3 and 8 inches, with locally higher amounts across the San Juan Mountains. Remnants from two tropical storms supplied anomalous moisture across the region, with minimal break between events. The first round of heavy rainfall resulted in flooding across La Plata and Archuleta counties from decaying Tropical Storm Priscilla beginning October 10 and continuing throughout the day on the 11th. There was a break in rain across the area on October 12, followed by a second round of heavy rain from October 13-14, associated with moisture from remnant Tropical Storm Raymond. Without much time between systems, the hydrologic response of the second storm was much faster than the first. Flooding across several counties in southwest Colorado spiked twice within the 5-day window, leading to major flooding and near record peaks along the Vallecito, Piedra and San Juan watersheds that lingered through the 16th before water levels receded. Numerous debris flows and rockslides also occurred as a result of the abundant rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain due to the remnants of Tropical Storm Priscilla falling between the evening of October 9th and October 11th, resulted in flooding along Vallecito Creek, which began rising around midnight on October 11th. Vallecito Creek crested at 1245 MST on October 11 to 5.99 feet and 7100 cfs, which is a new record historical peak, beating the previous record of 5.98 feet set on September 6 of 1970. The flood wave routed through the system through the evening of October 11th with streamflows dropping about 2 feet from the peak around 2200 on October 11th. There were 7 road closures, 600 to 700 residences were evacuated in Vallecito and there were 11 high water rescues in addition to threatened bridges. Debris was seen moving down the river including trees and objects from people's homes including a hot tub. Vallecito Creek remained elevated with a second crest of 4.38 feet around 800 MST on October 14 in response to another period of locally heavy rain and increased rates on October 13. The second flood wave routed through the system by 300 MST on October 16, before subsiding below bankfull levels. This second wave carved out a completely new channel moving at 3000 cfs.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant rainfall fell across the San Juan River Basin between the evening of October 9 and October 11 due to the remnants of Tropical Storm Priscilla. This resulted in major flooding along the San Juan River in Pagosa Springs. Action stage of 9.48 feet was exceeded on October 11 at 1100 MST. The San Juan River crested at major flood stage on October 11 at 1600 MST at 12.66 feet and 8270 cfs. The San Juan River then fell to near action stage on October 12 at 1000 MST. Four places were impacted in Archuleta County which were mostly rural. Most of the damage reported were dirt roads washed out and minor flooding to residents where the river exceeded its banks. There were numerous power outages and a water main broke in Pagosa Springs which was repaired on October 12. Preliminary estimates of $5 million to repair man-made white water rapids for kayaking and recreation along the San Juan River in Pagosa Springs and $2 million estimated to repair the bike path along the San Juan River in Pagosa Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between October 10-14, 2025, southwest Colorado experienced prolonged periods of rain and some of the largest multiday rainfall totals on record with rainfall accumulations between 3 and 8 inches, with locally higher amounts across the San Juan Mountains. Remnants from two tropical storms supplied anomalous moisture across the region, with minimal break between events. The first round of heavy rainfall resulted in flooding across La Plata and Archuleta counties from decaying Tropical Storm Priscilla beginning October 10 and continuing throughout the day on the 11th. There was a break in rain across the area on October 12, followed by a second round of heavy rain from October 13-14, associated with moisture from remnant Tropical Storm Raymond. Without much time between systems, the hydrologic response of the second storm was much faster than the first. Flooding across several counties in southwest Colorado spiked twice within the 5-day window, leading to major flooding and near record peaks along the Vallecito, Piedra and San Juan watersheds that lingered through the 16th before water levels receded. Numerous debris flows and rockslides also occurred as a result of the abundant rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Radar indicated heavy rain between 0300 and 0400 MST. This heavy rain caused multiple mudslides to occur around 0400 MST between Carbondale and Redstone along Highway 133 centered around mile marker 55.7. Another one occurred at mile marker 53 within this similar timeframe. One vehicle was stuck in the mudslide but not buried. No indication of how deep or wide the slide was but clean up took longer than usual due to the stuck vehicle. Road reopened in both directions at 0917 MST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Piedra River had a rapid rise in levels throughout the morning and afternoon of October 11th due to abundant rainfall starting on October 10th. It first crested to a preliminary level of 5.86 ft around midnight 10/12.  A second crest occurred to a preliminary level of 7.26 ft on 10/14 around 1300 MST. This set a new record, beating the previous crest record of 6.4 ft which was set back on 9/6/1970. Most issues were adjacent to the 151 corridor north of Arboles where numerous drives were under water, one garage had some water in it. Forest Service Rd 613 did have some inundation but most bridges had several feet of clearance. USFS 613 which is parallel to the river did have water in some areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Derby Fire started in mid August due to a lightning strike 13.5 miles north of Dotsero, Colorado. The fire grew to 5,453 acres spreading across Eagle and Garfield counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Derby Fire started in mid August due to a lightning strike 13.5 miles north of Dotsero, Colorado. The fire grew to 5,453 acres spreading across Eagle and Garfield counties. While major suppression efforts ended in October and significant fire activity ceased, the remaining 5 percent was in an inaccessible area with pockets of interior smoke. It was officially 100 percent contained by mid December. The estimated cost was $23,860,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnants of Tropical Storm Priscilla brought record-breaking moisture and widespread precipitation to western Colorado on October 10th and 11th. Portions of the region saturated under heavy rainfall with many locations experiencing periodic drops in visibility due to mist beginning early in the morning on the 10th and persisting into the early afternoon on the 11th. Dense fog was also able to develop overnight across the Grand Valley and in lower valleys along the I-70 corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread fog developed within the Grand Valley resulting from heavy rainfall on October 10 and 11. The KGJT ASOS at the Grand Junction Regional Airport reported sustained periods of reduced visibility from mist and fog beginning early in the morning on the 10th and persisting into the late morning on the 11th. The KGJT ASOS would report dense fog reducing visibility to 1/4 mile for about a half an hour shortly before midnight. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Anomalous moisture from Tropical Storm Priscilla remained across much of the western CONUS on the 11th, though drying mid-level air paved the way for clearing skies in the late afternoon. These clearer skies paired with a passing cold front lead to greatly cooler overnight lows. Widespread fog and mist developed across the region's valleys as a result, including dense fog along portions of the I-70 corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areas of fog developed within lower valleys along I-70 resulting from heavy rainfall on over the previous days. The KRIL ASOS at the Rifle Garfield County Airport reported sustained periods of reduced visibility from mist and fog throughout the night of the 11th and 12th. The KRIL ASOS would report a brief period of dense fog reducing visibility to less than 1/4 mile during the night. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak surface moisture moved through the region around a southern CONUS high pressure system. Skies remained clear due to a dry mid-level atmosphere though. Overnight temperatures fell to near or below freezing across much of northern Colorado as a result, leading to the formation of patchy dense fog.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Patchy fog developed over the Yampa River Basin during the morning of October 6. The KCAG ASOS at the Craig-Moffat County Airport reported decreasing visibility for about 4 hours overnight, including about half an hour of visibility dropping to or below 1/4 mile shortly after sunrise. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Downslope high wind event along the Front Range of Colorado produced wind damage and critical fire weather conditions. Peak wind gust estimated to reach 109 mph at the NCAR Mesa Lab, with several reports over 80 mph in/near the base of the foothills. Critical fire weather conditions resulted from the high winds and low humidity. Power poles were snapped along Highway 93 between Golden and Boulder. Total power outages were near 139,000, including the 50,000 in the PSPS. A plane was flipped due to high winds at the Boulder Municipal airport. Several trees were also snapped in/near the foothills, including some on the University of Colorado campus (which was closed for the day). Some closures of state highways occurred, some planned and unplanned, due to the strong winds. These included sections of US-287, CO-93, US-36, and CO-128. ||Over the northeast plains, a surge of west winds advanced eastward during the evening hours producing several gusts in the 55 to 75 mph range. The strongest wind gust on the plains during the evening hours was a 89 mph wind gust at the Akron airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather station and the NCAR Mesa Lab recorded a peak wind gust of 109 mph. There were many reports of 60 to 80 mph across the zone.  At 1 NE of Crisman (just NW of Boulder), a 93 mph wind gust was recorded. Several trees were snapped in/near the foothills. Highway 36 between Boulder and Lyons and Highway 287 between Ted's Place and the Wyoming border were closed due to the high winds.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm approached eastern Utah and western Colorado from the west, bringing a shot of colder air along with it. Ahead of this system, high pressure had brought a much warmer airmass. Between these two systems, and along and ahead of the approaching cold front, the surface pressure gradient began to tighten. Deep mixing ahead of the front also helped to bring down higher momentum air from the strong, 170 knot upper level jet. All together, this resulted in widespread strong, gusty winds across much of eastern Utah and western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Colorado Department of Transportation RWIS Sensor 082w023 Basalt-Holland Hills recorded a wind gust to 85 mph at 11:10 PM MST. This is in line with other gusts recorded in this elevation band during this window. Other gusts across the area ranged from 45 mph to 70 mph through the period. No reports of injuries, fatalities, or damages were received.

CZ_NAME: LARIMER COUNTY BELOW 6000 FEET / NW WELD COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Downslope high wind event along the Front Range of Colorado produced wind damage and critical fire weather conditions. Peak wind gust estimated to reach 109 mph at the NCAR Mesa Lab, with several reports over 80 mph in/near the base of the foothills. Critical fire weather conditions resulted from the high winds and low humidity. Power poles were snapped along Highway 93 between Golden and Boulder. Total power outages were near 139,000, including the 50,000 in the PSPS. A plane was flipped due to high winds at the Boulder Municipal airport. Several trees were also snapped in/near the foothills, including some on the University of Colorado campus (which was closed for the day). Some closures of state highways occurred, some planned and unplanned, due to the strong winds. These included sections of US-287, CO-93, US-36, and CO-128. ||Over the northeast plains, a surge of west winds advanced eastward during the evening hours producing several gusts in the 55 to 75 mph range. The strongest wind gust on the plains during the evening hours was a 89 mph wind gust at the Akron airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The strongest winds gusts measured were north of Fort Collins near the Buckeye area where a CDOT sensor measured an 82 mph wind gusts and a RAWS measured an 81 mph wind gusts during the early to mid evening hours. Several other reports of 60 to 80 mph were recorded in the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm approached eastern Utah and western Colorado from the west, bringing a shot of colder air along with it. Ahead of this system, high pressure had brought a much warmer airmass. Between these two systems, and along and ahead of the approaching cold front, the surface pressure gradient began to tighten. Deep mixing ahead of the front also helped to bring down higher momentum air from the strong, 170 knot upper level jet. All together, this resulted in widespread strong, gusty winds across much of eastern Utah and western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Colorado Department of Highways RWIS site 0.1 mi W of No Name Tunnel recorded a gust to 60 mph at 9:40 PM MST. Additional gusts across the region ranged from 45 mph to 55 mph, with the highest gust recorded at an ASOS or AWOS being 46 mph at KEGE AWOS at Eagle County Regional Airport. No reports of injuries, fatalities, or damages were received.

CZ_NAME: LARIMER COUNTY BELOW 6000 FEET / NW WELD COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme winds and fire danger occurred during the morning and afternoon hours of December 19th due to a strong mountain wave. The high winds occurred in and near the foothills of Larimer, Boulder, and Jefferson. The eastward extent of the high winds only reach the western parts of the Denver Metro area and stayed west of Interstate 25. Several (five) fires were reported in the foothills. Numerous power poles and power lines were reported down (at least 28 different reports) across Boulder and the immediate foothills as peak wind gusts hit 90-110 mph. In Gilpin County, there were 100s of trees down and several overhead powerlines down along Gap Road. Several road closures were also noted (some proactively). Public Safety Power Shutoff (PSPS) was enacted early to mid morning by Xcel Energy to approximately 70,000 customers, and a total of approximately 104,000 customers were without power due to additional unplanned outages. The Jefferson County EM reported that there were evacuations for Windy Peak Outdoor Lab, part of the Jefferson County school district. There were difficulties evacuating staff from this location due to the number of downed trees. FlightAware reported more than 1,200 delays at DIA along with more than 80 cancellations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gust of 95 mph was recorded 3 miles south of Carter Lake. There were many wind gusts in the 60 to 80 mph range as well.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second upper level low pressure trough and cold front moved through the area on the heels of the previous one at the beginning of December, bringing moderate snowfall to the central and southern mountains of western Colorado and eastern Utah. As this trough moved through the Four Corners, light to moderate snow also fell across portions of the southwest Colorado valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 3 to 8 inches of snow fell across the area with 8.5 inches of snow reported at Vail 2.1 ENE.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong plume of atmospheric river moisture arrived on the morning of December 6 from the northwest, bringing rain and snow showers to portions of the Colorado west slope. Overnight, the moisture plume descended southwards, leaving west-central and southwestern Colorado valleys to saturate. Dense fog was able to develop before and near sunrise in these valleys as a result, though poor radiational cooling from cloudy skies kept fog relatively localized and short-lived.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areas of dense fog developed along the high-elevation valleys along I-70 during the morning of December 7. The KEGE AWOS at the Eagle County Regional Airport reported two periods of 1/4-mile visibility due to freezing fog. The first occurrence was before sunrise and brief, lasting less than thirty minutes. The second occurrence lasted about an hour, beginning near sunrise. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwesterly flow brought enhanced moisture and light snow showers to northwestern Colorado on December 10. Overnight into December 11, high pressure moved overhead, leaving clearer skies and cooling temperatures. High cloud cover mildly hampered radiational cooling, keeping dense fog duration predominantly short-lived over high-elevation valleys where surface moisture lingered.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Localized dense fog developed within the Upper Gunnison River Valley on the morning of December 11. The KGUC AWOS at the Gunnison���Crested Butte Regional Airport reported multiple periods of reduced visibility due to fog. For roughly two hours beginning near sunset, 1/4-mile visibility was reported. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest flow across the southwestern CONUS transported Pacific moisture into southwestern Colorado on December 25. Rain showers fell over much of the San Juans and nearby river basins as a result. Overnight into December 26, pockets of drier mid-level air developed over the same areas which had received rainfall. Clearer skies developed as dense cloud cover moved to the northwest and southeast of the San Juans. Favorable radiational cooling paired with the remaining surface moisture supported the development of dense fog in many of the valleys of southwestern Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Localized dense fog developed within the Animas River Basin on the morning of December 26. The KDRO ASOS at the Durango-La Plata County Airport reported multiple prolonged periods of reduced visibility throughout the night due to mist and fog, though dense fog with visibility of 1/4 mile only briefly developed, lasting less than 30 minutes. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

CZ_NAME: BOULDER & JEFFERSON COUNTIES BELOW 6000 FEET / W BROOMFIELD COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme winds and fire danger occurred during the morning and afternoon hours of December 19th due to a strong mountain wave. The high winds occurred in and near the foothills of Larimer, Boulder, and Jefferson. The eastward extent of the high winds only reach the western parts of the Denver Metro area and stayed west of Interstate 25. Several (five) fires were reported in the foothills. Numerous power poles and power lines were reported down (at least 28 different reports) across Boulder and the immediate foothills as peak wind gusts hit 90-110 mph. In Gilpin County, there were 100s of trees down and several overhead powerlines down along Gap Road. Several road closures were also noted (some proactively). Public Safety Power Shutoff (PSPS) was enacted early to mid morning by Xcel Energy to approximately 70,000 customers, and a total of approximately 104,000 customers were without power due to additional unplanned outages. The Jefferson County EM reported that there were evacuations for Windy Peak Outdoor Lab, part of the Jefferson County school district. There were difficulties evacuating staff from this location due to the number of downed trees. FlightAware reported more than 1,200 delays at DIA along with more than 80 cancellations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many gusts reached 70 to 90 mph between Boulder and Longmont. The strongest gust, 102 mph, was recorded just west of the Niwot. In Longmont, the windstorm severely damaged the roof of Longmont Climbing Collective, 155 Pinnacle Street, prompting the closure of the business as crews work to replace the roof.  On the University of Colorado Boulder campus, branches littered the ground along with some large trees that were uprooted. Some roofs were also damage with shingles being removed. Accompanying these strong winds were relative humidities of 5 to 15 percent.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest flow across the southwestern CONUS transported Pacific moisture into southwestern Colorado on December 25. Rain showers fell over much of the San Juans and nearby river basins as a result. Overnight into December 26, pockets of drier mid-level air developed over the same areas which had received rainfall. Clearer skies developed as dense cloud cover moved to the northwest and southeast of the San Juans. Favorable radiational cooling paired with the remaining surface moisture supported the development of dense fog in many of the valleys of southwestern Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areas of dense fog developed within the San Juan River Basin overnight from December 25 through 26. The KPSO AWOS at the Stevens-Pagosa Springs Airfield reported multiple prolonged periods of reduced visibility throughout the night due to mist and fog with numerous occurrences of 1/4-mile visibility from dense frog, each lasting 15 minutes to 1 hour. Fog dissipated shortly after sunrise. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought high wind gusts up to 83 mph across Yuma county through just past midnight on the 18th. A couple wildfires occurred as well due to winds blowing down power lines. The largest fire was near Heartstrong which burned around 12,000 acres. Additional wind gusts to 76 mph was recorded in Burlington, along with a 63 mph wind gust near Cheyenne Wells in Cheyenne county.  The high winds caused property damage in northern Yuma County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Yuma county emergency manager relayed that multiple fires were ongoing across the county as wind gusts of 60-83 mph were ongoing behind a cold front. There were also three smoke plumes on radar as well. The largest was named 33 Fire as it was along CR 33 near the town of Heartstrong, Colorado. It is believed the fire was started from downed power lines from the wind. The fire burned 12,000 acres but with the only damage known to be to an abandoned trailer and a pig barn with no livestock inside. The other fires from that day burned around 2000 acres near CR L in Yuma county and 50 acres near CR 44. Near the intersection of CR T and 28 a 15x20 chicken building with 20 chickens, 9 ducks and 1 rabbit was lost along with some minor damage to a goat pen. Fire damage was also reported to sprinklers and fence line near the intersection of CR 27 and CR U. A vacant house was burnt down as well near the same intersection.

CZ_NAME: S & E JACKSON / LARIMER / N & NE GRAND / NW BOULDER COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Downslope high wind event along the Front Range of Colorado produced wind damage and critical fire weather conditions. Peak wind gust estimated to reach 109 mph at the NCAR Mesa Lab, with several reports over 80 mph in/near the base of the foothills. Critical fire weather conditions resulted from the high winds and low humidity. Power poles were snapped along Highway 93 between Golden and Boulder. Total power outages were near 139,000, including the 50,000 in the PSPS. A plane was flipped due to high winds at the Boulder Municipal airport. Several trees were also snapped in/near the foothills, including some on the University of Colorado campus (which was closed for the day). Some closures of state highways occurred, some planned and unplanned, due to the strong winds. These included sections of US-287, CO-93, US-36, and CO-128. ||Over the northeast plains, a surge of west winds advanced eastward during the evening hours producing several gusts in the 55 to 75 mph range. The strongest wind gust on the plains during the evening hours was a 89 mph wind gust at the Akron airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gunsight RAWS just west of this zone reported a 85 mph wind gust. There are a lack of observations for this mountainous zone. Given the 85 report nearby and others, this area experienced high winds for several hours on the 17th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front brought high wind gusts up to 83 mph across Yuma county through just past midnight on the 18th. A couple wildfires occurred as well due to winds blowing down power lines. The largest fire was near Heartstrong which burned around 12,000 acres. Additional wind gusts to 76 mph was recorded in Burlington, along with a 63 mph wind gust near Cheyenne Wells in Cheyenne county.  The high winds caused property damage in northern Yuma County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The strongest measured wind gust of the event was recorded 3 W Wray via a WeatherUnderground mesonet site named KCOWRAY25. The site also recorded a 81mph wind gust just before the 83mph gust. Another 80 mph wind gust was recorded via RAWS station near Hale named TT563 South Republican. A 75 mph wind gust was also recorded 4 NE Idalia on CDOT site CO047. A 71 mph wind gust was recorded on the Wray Airport AWOS named K2V5, a 64 mph wind gust was recorded shortly before as well. An Ambient Weather site 6 NNW Wauneta recorded a 69 mph wind gust. A CoAgMet site named CSU50 located 8 ENE Clarkville recorded a 60 mph wind gust. The county Emergency Manager relayed numerous damage reports such as numerous reports of roof and damage was reported in the town of Wray. A private hangar at the city of Wray Airport was destroyed due to the wind. Debris from this hangar also damaged neighboring hangars to the south. Fencing and part of a wood frame building was damaged in Eckley. A large tree in the town of Wray fell on a house destroying the house and compromising the framing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hay bale fire occurred to the west of Burlington, Colorado on the morning of the 18th. 50-100 hay bales were damaged or lost to the fire. Smoke damage was reported to a house directly north of where the fire was at about 500 yards or so.  Smoke from the fire also caused the local school to start late.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A haybale fire occurred to the west of Burlington, Colorado during the morning of the 18th. Roughly 50-100 haybales were damaged or lost due to the fire. Some smoke damage also occurred to a residence roughly 500 yards to the north of the fire. The local high school had a late start due to the smoke being too hazardous for the kids to be in the building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winds steadily increased through the day of 25th in the wake of an early morning cold front. The strongest wind gust recorded was a 62 mph gust in northern Yuma county 10 ENE Clarkville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 62 mph wind gust was recorded on Ambient Weather site Struckmeyer which is located 10 ENE Clarkville. A gust of 56 mph was recorded on the same station at 10:03am MT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The month of November was overall warmer than normal across eastern Utah and western Colorado, but was wetter or drier than normal depending on where you stood. Additionally, much of the area still saw benefits from the well above normal precipitation that occurred in October. As a result, only minor changes to drought of Severe (D2), Moderate (D1), or Abnormally Dry (D0) categories occurred with each update, but the area of Extreme (D3) Drought remained unchanged through the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the beginning of the month of November, the US Drought Monitor analyzed an area of Extreme (D3) Drought as covering roughly 30% of Colorado Zone 008 - Central Colorado River Basin. The remainder of the zone was under Severe (D2) conditions. These conditions remained unchanged through the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The month of November was overall warmer than normal across eastern Utah and western Colorado, but was wetter or drier than normal depending on where you stood. Additionally, much of the area still saw benefits from the well above normal precipitation that occurred in October. As a result, only minor changes to drought of Severe (D2), Moderate (D1), or Abnormally Dry (D0) categories occurred with each update, but the area of Extreme (D3) Drought remained unchanged through the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The US Drought Monitor removed the last area of Extreme (D3) Drought from Colorado Zone 009 - Grand and Battlement Mesas on October 14th, 2025. Extreme (D3) Drought did not reoccur for a month. Therefore this will be the last entry for this zone until such time as Extreme (D3) Drought redevelops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Broad southwesterly flow was in place over the area, which allowed for high terrain thunderstorms to develop. With adequate shear and instability in place, a supercell tornado developed across Teller County, with a landspout tornado within the Wet Mountain Valley in Custer County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A damage assessment was performed for an EF1 tornado that formed at 1:55PM MDT on July 30th, 2025 6 miles WNW of Woodland Park, Colorado. The path length was .8 miles oriented from northwest to southwest and had a maximum width of 75 yards. There was extensive tree damage with the primary damage indicator being widespread uprooted softwood trees and an occasional snapped softwood tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of severe storms pushed eastward across the eastern plains late in the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured wind gusts of 76 mph occurred in north central Prowers County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough brought rain and snowfall to the San Juan mountains on November 23. Overnight, the trough moved out of the region. A weak pressure gradient made for calmer winds, and a drying mid-level atmosphere cleared skies. With the recent precipitation though, low-level moisture remained pooled over southwestern Colorado leading to the development of widespread dense fog.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areas of dense fog developed within the upper valleys of the San Juan mountains overnight from November 23 to 24. The KTEX AWOS at the Telluride Regional Airport reported three periods of 1/4-mile visibility due to dense freezing fog throughout the night, each lasting less than two hours. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread dense fog developed within the Animas River Basin near and just after sunrise on November 24. The KDRO ASOS at the Durango-La Plata County Airport reported multiple hours of 1/4-mile visibility due to freezing fog, and multiple periods up to 30 minutes long of visibility reduced to less that 1/4 mile. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwesterly flow ahead of a trough brought increased moisture to southwestern Colorado. Terrain-driven showers fell over portions of the San Juan mountains as a result. Falling overnight temperatures paired with the increased moisture promoted widespread dense fog over high-elevation valleys in the San Juans.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areas of dense fog developed within high-elevation valleys of the San Juan mountains resulting from multiple hours of snowfall during the early overnight period. The KTEX AWOS at the Telluride Regional Airport reported about four hours of 1/4-mile visibility from freezing fog around sunrise on the 20th. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A zone of deformation set up over the region ahead of a closed low, but a deep moist layer remained across the west slope of the Rockies. Overcast skies during the afternoon of November 20 kept the cool temperatures and near-saturated environment in place. This supported another round of snow showers early in the night of the 20th and the subsequent development of widespread dense fog.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread, persistent dense fog developed within high-elevation valleys in the San Juan mountains following a round of terrain-driven snow showers. The KTEX AWOS at the Telluride Regional Airport reported multiple hours of 1/4-mile visibility from freezing fog throughout the majority of the night. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Patches of dense fog developed over portions of the San Juan River Basin following a round of snow showers in the early morning hours on November 21. The KPSO AWOS at the Stevens-Pagosa Springs Airfield reported two brief periods of 1/4-mile visibility from freezing fog before sunset. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system hanging around Southern California for a few days lifted across the Great Basin and across southwest Colorado, bringing a surge of modest atmospheric river moisture with it. Warm antecedent temperatures resulted in higher snow levels with mostly rain in lower elevations.  Light to moderate snow accumulations occurred aross the central and southern mountains of western Colorado with areas of blowing snow as well due to strong winds above 12,000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 10 inches of snow fell across the area. A peak wind gust of 47 mph was recorded at Storm Peak Lab.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a low passing to our south, calm winds and clearing skies began to dominate the region. Though the mid-level atmosphere was quickly drying out, low-level moisture persisted in lower valleys across the western slope of the Rockies. Patchy fog was able to develop across portions of northwestern Colorado as a result, and widespread fog developed over portions of southwestern Colorado before quickly burning up after sunrise.

The KCAG ASOS at the Craig-Moffatt County Airport reported very brief periods of 1/4-mile visibility within the span of an hour just after sunrise due to freezing fog. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of 1.5-2.5 inches in 30 min. led to flash flooding in Manitou Springs. Excess runoff and minor inundation of roadways occurred for most areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough brought rain and snowfall to the San Juan mountains on November 23. Overnight, the trough moved out of the region. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure trough and associated cold front dropped southward out of the Pacific Northwest across the Great Basin and through eastern Utah and western Colorado. Moderate to heavy snowfall favored the central and southern mountains with higher amounts along the frontal boundary draped across the central mountains, La Sal Mountains and San Juan mountains. Gusty winds in the 45 to 55 mph range above 12,000 feet also contributed to blowing snow with the forcing from the jet dynamics and cold frontal boundary with banded snow as well.  Most lower valleys remained limited with snowfall due to higher snow levels with exception of the Paonia to Hotchkiss area that benefited from banding along the front and convection helping drive snow levels lower at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 to 12 inches of snow fell across the area with 7.5 inches reported at Ridgway 3.6 NW and up to 12 inches at Columbine Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Messy westerly to northwesterly flow was in place over the area, which allowed for widespread storms to develop, some of which became strong to severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report from mPING. Hail measured to 1.25 inches occurred in Bent County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak northwesterly flow was in place over the area, which allowed for scattered storms to develop across south central Colorado. A couple of these storms became severe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Messy flow was in place over the area, allowing for scattered storm clusters to develop, especially along the Palmer Divide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Messy westerly flow was in place once again, allowing for scattered storms to develop across the area, some of which become strong to severe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Derby Fire started in mid August due to a lightning strike 13.5 miles north of Dotsero, Colorado. The fire grew to 5,453 acres spreading across Eagle and Garfield counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Derby Fire started in mid August due to a lightning strike 13.5 miles north of Dotsero, Colorado. The fire grew to 5,453 acres spreading across Eagle and Garfield counties. While major suppression efforts ended in October and significant fire activity ceased, the remaining 5 percent was in an inaccessible area with pockets of interior smoke. It was officially 100 percent contained by mid December. The estimated cost was $23,860,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Broad southwesterly flow was in place over the area, which allowed for high terrain thunderstorms to develop. With adequate shear and instability in place, a supercell tornado developed across Teller County, with a landspout tornado within the Wet Mountain Valley in Custer County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brief landspout tornado developed over an open field in Custer County. 3 power poles were damaged. A virtual damage assessment was completed with a rating of EF-0 and 85 MPH max wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed low pressure system over Southern California lifted northeast towards the Four Corners and tracked across southeast Utah and southwest Colorado as an open wave. This resulted in light to moderate snowfall accumulations favoring the southwest San Juan Mountains with much less snow elsewhere.

Extreme (D3) Drought did not reoccur for a month. Therefore this will be the last entry for this zone until such time as Extreme (D3) Drought redevelops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A drying atmosphere was established over western Colorado underneath high pressure ahead of a shortwave trough. In spite of this, a pool of surface moisture remained trapped within southwestern Colorado valleys due to a December 2 storm which dropped several inches of snow over the San Juan Mountains. The low-level environment here remained near-saturated for several days as a result. Reduced visibility from mist and fog was exhibited in the nights prior, but cooler overnight low temperatures led to dense fog development and 1/4-mile visibility overnight from December 4-5.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areas of dense fog developed within the Animas River Basin overnight on December 4-5. The KDRO ASOS at the Durango La-Plata County Airport reported over two hours of 1/4-mile visibility due to freezing fog. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure trough and associated cold front dropped southward out of the Pacific Northwest across the Great Basin and through eastern Utah and western Colorado. Moderate to heavy snowfall favored the central and southern mountains with higher amounts along the frontal boundary draped across the central mountains, La Sal Mountains and San Juan mountains. Gusty winds in the 45 to 55 mph range above 12,000 feet also contributed to blowing snow with the forcing from the jet dynamics and cold frontal boundary with banded snow as well. Most lower valleys remained limited with snowfall due to higher snow levels with exception of the Paonia to Hotchkiss area that benefited from banding along the front and convection helping drive snow levels lower at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 5 to 10 inches of snow fell across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Amplified troughing was in place over the region, bringing unseasonably strong shear to the area. A brief tornado develop along the northern slopes of Sawtooth Mountain in Saguache County as a supercell thunderstorm passed across the Continental Divide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado developed along the northern slopes of Sawtooth |Mountain during the early afternoon of September 13th, 2025. This |tornado was at approximately 11,300ft in elevation. It quickly |pushed eastward causing tree damage and then dissipated after |about 5 minutes. Images from Saguache County Emergency Management |and a damage scar recognized via satellite data, were used to |perform the damage survey. Both radar data and the environment |were supportive of a tornado at this location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong plume of atmospheric river moisture arrived on the morning of December 6 from the northwest, bringing rain and snow showers to portions of the Colorado west slope. Overnight, the moisture plume descended southwards, leaving west-central and southwestern Colorado valleys to saturate. Dense fog was able to develop before and near sunrise in these valleys as a result, though poor radiational cooling from cloudy skies kept fog relatively localized and short-lived.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Localized dense fog developed in the San Juan River Basin during the morning of December 7. The KPSO AWOS at the Stevens-Pagosa Springs Airfield reported about thirty minutes of freezing fog reducing visibility to 1/4 mile shortly after sunrise. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwesterly flow ahead of an upper-level trough brought snow showers to much of northwestern Colorado throughout December 27. Surface conditions became saturated with the cooling evening temperatures, leading to the development of dense fog across portions of northwest Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog due to snow showers developed within the Central Yampa River Basin on the evening of December 27. The KHDN AWOS at the Yampa Valley Regional Airport reported reduced visibility due to fog occurring between snow showers. Dense fog occurred for about 30 minutes during this period, with visibility dropping to less than 1/4 mile for about five minutes within that span. No reports of injuries, damages, or fatalities were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Derby Fire started in mid August due to a lightning strike 13.5 miles north of Dotsero, Colorado. The fire grew to 5,453 acres spreading across Eagle and Garfield counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Derby Fire started in mid August due to a lightning strike 13.5 miles north of Dotsero, Colorado. The fire grew to 5,453 acres spreading across Eagle and Garfield counties. While major suppression efforts ended in October and significant fire activity ceased, the remaining 5 percent was in an inaccessible area with pockets of interior smoke. It was officially 100 percent contained by mid December. The estimated cost was $23,860,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The month of December was generally uneventful as far as drought was concerned. Most locations remained below the Extreme (D3) Drought threshold, with only areas of the central and northern Divide mountains seeing conditions worsen. Throughout the winter season, and even back into the Fall, this area has received significantly below normal precipitation, resulting in the worsening drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the beginning of the month of December, the US Drought Monitor analyzed an area of Extreme (D3) Drought conditions in Colorado Zone 001 - Lower Yampa River Basin. This area comprised around 3% of the zone, with 27% of the zone under Severe (D2) Drought conditions, and the remainder under Moderate (D1) Drought conditions. These drought conditions did not change through the end of the month.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the beginning of the month of December the US Drought Monitor analyzed an area of Extreme (D3) Drought in Colorado Zone 008 - Central Colorado River Basin. This area comprised roughly 25% of the zone, with the remainder under Severe (D2) Drought conditions. As of the 12/24 Drought Monitor update, Exceptional (D4) Drought developed in the zone, covering roughly 5% of the zone, with 20% now under Extreme (D3) Drought conditions, and the remaining 75% still under Severe (D2) Drought conditions. With the 12/31 update, the area of Exceptional (D4) Drought area remained at 5% coverage, but the area of Exception (D4) Drought expanded to cover 40% of the zone. The remaining 55% of the zone was under Severe (D2) Drought.
